28.reply:
First: breathe. This is the fixable kind of bad news, not the other kind. Volunteer-run clubs get their books wrong constantly, tax offices know it, and an honest mistake you're voluntarily correcting is a completely different animal from something they catch in an audit. You didn't cause this and you're already doing the responsible thing by looking at it.

Before you spiral about a tax bill, one question changes everything: is your club even liable for income tax? A lot of small community sporting clubs aren't. In Australia a non-profit set up to encourage a sport can self-assess as income-tax exempt, which would mean "under-reported income" doesn't create an income tax debt at all. The real exposure in that case is usually GST, PAYG withholding on any paid staff, or reporting obligations, not tax on the income itself. So the first job isn't panicking about how much you owe; it's working out what obligation was actually breached, if any.

Here's what I'd actually do, in order:

1. Don't touch the old records to "fix" them. Preserve the bank statements, prior lodgments, and the previous treasurer's files exactly as they are. Retroactively editing history is the one move that turns a mistake into something that looks like a cover-up.

2. Tell the committee. You're a volunteer, not the club. This is the committee's problem to own and authorise, and you want it minuted that you flagged it and they agreed on the fix. That protects the club and it protects you personally.

3. Get a registered tax agent or accountant who works with not-for-profits, ideally before you contact the tax office. It's cheap against the risk, and a disclosure that comes through an agent, with the numbers already worked out, lands far better than a panicked phone call.

4. Make a voluntary disclosure. This is the single biggest lever you have. Coming forward before they come to you slashes penalties, and for genuine errors it often wipes them entirely; penalties scale with how culpable you were and whether you volunteered it. Two years of honest bookkeeping errors, self-reported, is close to the best-case version of this.

On you personally: a volunteer treasurer who inherited a predecessor's honest mistakes and moved to fix them is not the person who ends up in trouble. The exposure grows if it gets buried, not if it gets disclosed. Fixing it is the thing that keeps you clean.

You don't have to sort this tonight. Do it properly over the next week or two.

One thing that'd help me get concrete: are you in Australia, and roughly how big is the club (does it have paid staff, is it GST-registered, does it lodge a tax return at all)? Tell me that and I'll pull up the specific ATO self-assessment rules so you know exactly which box you're in before you spend a cent on an accountant.

29.reply:
Breathe. This is fixable, and the fact that you found it and want to fix it puts you in a much better spot than you feel like you're in right now.

Here's the honest version: tax offices deal with this constantly. Bookkeeping errors by a well-meaning volunteer who was in over their head are about the most ordinary thing in the world to them. What they care about is what you do now that you know. Nobody's coming to arrest the new treasurer of a footy club because the last one couldn't reconcile a bank statement.

The single most important thing: you come to them before they come to you. It's called a voluntary disclosure, and if you're in Australia the ATO has a formal process for exactly this. The difference in how you get treated is enormous. Correct it yourself and the penalties are typically reduced heavily or waived entirely, and it's usually just the tax owed plus some interest. Wait for an audit to find it and that goodwill evaporates. So the clock working in your favour is the clock where you're the one moving.

What I'd actually do, roughly in this order:

Don't touch or "tidy up" anything in the old records yet. Not out of paranoia, just because you'll want the real picture and a clean trail showing you found honest mistakes, not that you papered over them.

Pin down the scope before you tell anyone official. How wrong, over which periods, and roughly how much income went unreported. You don't need it perfect, you need it defensible. If the club's turnover is small this might be a weekend of going through bank statements against what was lodged.

Get a registered tax agent or accountant involved, ideally one who's worked with small clubs or not-for-profits. This is the step people skip because volunteer clubs run on no money, and it's the one that pays for itself. They'll know whether the club even owed tax on some of this (a lot of community sports clubs have income that's exempt or mutual, and you may find the "under-reporting" is smaller than it looks once someone who knows the rules goes through it). They also make the disclosure for you, in the right language, to the right part of the tax office.

Tell your committee. You're a volunteer, and this predates you. It should never sit on your shoulders alone, and the committee needs to formally back the decision to disclose and to spend a bit on an accountant. Get it minuted. That protects you personally, too.

A couple of things to take the edge off the panic. You almost certainly have no personal liability here for an honest predecessor's errors, especially once you've acted properly on finding it. And "two years of errors" from a volunteer is small potatoes to a tax office, not fraud. Fraud is deliberate concealment. This is the opposite of that, you're literally trying to hand them the correct numbers.

One caveat, because I'd rather be useful than confident: I don't know which country you're in or your club's exact tax status, and those change the specifics. The direction of travel is the same everywhere though, get the facts, get an accountant, disclose voluntarily, keep your committee in the loop.

You're doing the right thing by not sitting on it. If you tell me the country and rough size of the club, I can get more specific about the actual process and forms.

30.reply:
First: you're not in trouble. Take that in before you read anything else, because panic is going to make you do something rushed and the rushed thing is usually the wrong one.

Here's the honest read. A volunteer treasurer who inherits someone else's mess and reports it the moment they find it is about the safest position you can be in with a tax office. The people who get hammered are the ones who knew and hid it. That's not you. Two years of a predecessor's bad bookkeeping is a boringly common situation, and there's a well-worn path for exactly this.

The single most important concept is voluntary disclosure. If you go to the tax office and tell them before they come to you, penalties drop hard, sometimes to nothing on the penalty side, and they're far more willing to work out a payment arrangement on any actual tax owed. Getting ahead of it is the whole game. So don't sit on this, but also don't fire off a panicked email tonight.

Do this in order.

Stop and don't contact the tax office yet. Not because you're hiding anything, but because you need real numbers first. Walking in with "I think something's wrong" invites a broader look than walking in with "here are the two corrected years and the exact shortfall."

Pull the records. Bank statements, the old books, receipts, whatever the previous treasurer left. Work out roughly how big the gap actually is across those two years. It might be smaller than the dread in your stomach is telling you.

One thing worth checking early, because it could change everything: is your club actually liable for income tax at all? A lot of community sporting clubs qualify as non-profits set up to encourage a sport and are income-tax exempt. If that's your club, "under-reported income" might be a GST issue, or a reporting-format issue, rather than a tax debt. Different problem, usually a smaller one. Don't assume the worst number is the real number.

Get a registered tax agent or accountant, ideally one who's handled not-for-profits or clubs. This is not a DIY moment. Many will do a community sports club cheap or free, and the committee can approve the cost. They'll quantify the gap properly, tell you whether you're exempt, and handle the disclosure wording so it lands well.

Tell your committee now, in writing. You should not be carrying this alone, and you shouldn't be the only person who knows. Get it minuted: what you found, when you found it, and that the committee resolved to correct it and disclose. That paper trail protects you personally and shows good faith if anyone ever asks. It's a club liability, not your personal one, as long as you're acting properly and not benefiting yourself.

On your personal exposure: as an unpaid volunteer acting in good faith and reporting the error, you're extremely unlikely to be personally liable for the club's tax. The thing that changes that is knowing and covering it up. So the protective move and the honest move are the same move here, which is convenient.

What I'd actually do this week: message the committee chair today with a short, calm "I've found a bookkeeping problem from before my time, I'm getting it sorted properly, we need a quick meeting." Then spend the weekend gathering records. Then get an accountant in front of it before you contact anyone official.

I'm not your accountant and I don't know your club's exact tax status or which country's rules you're under, so treat this as the shape of the plan, not the final legal word. But the shape is right, and the shape starts with: breathe, document, get help, disclose. You caught someone else's mistake and you're fixing it. That's the good version of this story, not the bad one.

1.reply:
First, breathe. This is fixable, and what you're describing is something the tax office sees all the time: an honest bookkeeping error, found by the person trying to do the right thing. That is a very different situation from someone hiding money, and it gets treated very differently. You're the one who caught it, and you didn't cause it.

The most important thing to understand is that the tax office treats a mistake you disclose to them *voluntarily*, before they come asking, far more leniently than one they find themselves. I'm assuming you're in Australia and this is the ATO (tell me if it's somewhere else, because the specifics change). A voluntary disclosure made before any audit or review usually cuts the penalties heavily, and where reasonable care was genuinely taken it can reduce them close to nothing. Interest on the shortfall may still apply, but that's a money figure to be worked out, not a scandal. So the whole direction here is to get ahead of it rather than wait.

The one thing not to do is quietly alter or backdate the old records to make them look correct. That single move is what turns an honest error into something that looks like a cover-up. Leave the history as it stands; you fix it going forward and through a proper disclosure.

Before you assume there's even a large bill: check whether the club is actually liable for income tax at all. Many small community sports clubs are not-for-profits that are either income-tax exempt or only taxed on a narrow part of their income. If that's your club, "under-reported income" may mean the paperwork was wrong but little or no tax was ever really owed. That check alone can shrink this from a crisis to a correction, so do it early.

And this genuinely isn't yours to carry alone. You're the volunteer treasurer who inherited someone else's mistake, and how the club responds is a committee decision, not a weight for you to hold by yourself. You're also very unlikely to be personally on the hook for a predecessor's honest errors, especially when you act properly the moment you find them.

Concretely, the next steps in order:

1. Tell the committee or president now, plainly, so the club is deciding this together.
2. Get a bookkeeper or accountant to work out how much income was under-reported across the two years, so you're dealing in real numbers.
3. Ask that accountant or a registered tax agent whether tax was actually owed and how to lodge a voluntary disclosure.
4. Lodge the disclosure before the ATO ever contacts you.

You found a problem and you're moving to fix it correctly. That's exactly the position you want to be in with a tax authority. Tell me the club's setup (incorporated association? registered not-for-profit? roughly how much money moves through it a year?) and I can help you figure out whether tax was even owed and how to word the disclosure.

2.reply:
Take a breath. This is a fixable problem, and what you're describing is not the situation that gets people in serious trouble. Honest bookkeeping errors that you inherited and are now trying to correct are treated very differently from deliberate concealment, in Australia and almost everywhere else. What determines how this goes is what you do next, and by taking it seriously you're already doing the right thing.

The single most important principle: tax authorities go much easier on people who come to them before they come to you. The ATO, like most tax offices, has a voluntary disclosure process built exactly for this. Correcting past errors before any audit typically reduces or removes penalties and can cut the interest too. So your goal is an orderly, documented correction, not a scramble to hide anything.

What to actually do, roughly in order:

1. Don't alter or "fix" the old records. Preserve them exactly as they are and make copies. The trail of what happened is what protects you.
2. Take this to your committee or board now, and get it minuted. You're a volunteer acting in good faith; this is the club's problem to own, and the minutes are your proof that you raised it promptly.
3. Get the real numbers before contacting anyone. You need to know roughly how much income was under-reported and over which periods. If the books are messy, pay a bookkeeper or accountant for a few hours. A professional reconstruction is far more credible than your best guess.
4. Have an accountant help frame the voluntary disclosure before you approach the tax office. How it's presented matters, and someone who does this regularly will know the exact process and any relief a small club qualifies for.

Two honest caveats. I'm not an accountant and I can't be certain of your jurisdiction. If the club isn't in Australia the principle still holds, but confirm the local process. And it's worth pinning down what "errors" means: genuine mistakes are one thing, but if the numbers suggest money actually went missing rather than was just misrecorded, that's a more serious conversation, and you'd want professional advice before doing anything.

You didn't cause this, and you caught it. That's the treasurer doing exactly the job.

3.reply:
First: breathe. This is recoverable, and it is far more ordinary than it feels at this moment. Tax offices deal with exactly this all the time, and honest bookkeeping errors are treated completely differently from deliberate evasion — that distinction is the whole ballgame, and it's firmly on your side.

The single most important thing to understand is the power of disclosing it yourself before they ever contact you. In most tax systems — and if you're in Australia, this is the ATO's voluntary disclosure process specifically — coming forward voluntarily about a genuine mistake sharply reduces penalties, and for honest errors it often reduces them to little or nothing. You'd still owe the underpaid tax (and possibly some interest), but that's a correction, not a punishment. That one move is your strongest protection, so protect it: don't let anything happen that looks like concealment.

Which means the things *not* to do are as important as the things to do. Don't hide it, don't alter, backdate, or delete any records, and don't quietly "fix" future returns while hoping the past goes unnoticed — that's the one path that turns an honest predecessor's mistake into something that could actually land on you. Also don't try to pin down the exact figures on your own; you need real numbers, not estimates, before anyone talks to the tax office.

And hear this clearly: you didn't do this. You inherited it, you caught it, and your job now is only to fix it cleanly — not to have prevented it. A volunteer treasurer acting in good faith and correcting an error is in a fundamentally safe position. The club is the taxpayer here, not you personally.

The path forward, in order:

1. Get the records together and bring in a bookkeeper or accountant to quantify the actual under-reported income across the two years. You want firm figures before any official conversation.
2. Tell your committee or board, and get it minuted. This is the club's liability and its decision to make — you should not be carrying it alone, and the minutes also document that the moment you knew, you acted.
3. Make the voluntary disclosure and amend the affected returns, ideally with the accountant drafting how it's worded and dealing with the tax office directly.

The accountant is worth the money — not because the situation is dire, but because having a professional handle the disclosure and the numbers is what makes this a clean, low-stress correction rather than a thing you lie awake over.

One thing I'm assuming: that you're in Australia, given the "tax office" phrasing — the voluntary-disclosure principle holds across most tax systems, but the exact process and penalty relief depend on where the club is registered, so confirm that with whoever you bring in.

4.reply:
First — take a breath. This is fixable, it's an ordinary kind of problem, and you are not in the trouble your gut is telling you you're in right now. Tax offices deal with exactly this all the time: a volunteer inherits messy books and finds honest mistakes. That is a completely different animal from someone deliberately hiding income, and it gets treated completely differently.

Here's the thing that matters most, and it's genuinely on your side: when you find an inadvertent error and you go to them, that's a *voluntary disclosure*. Penalties for honest mistakes that you report yourself are typically reduced heavily or waived — the whole system is built to reward people for putting their hand up. What you want to avoid is the version where they find it first. You're nowhere near that. You found it, and you're already trying to fix it. That's the responsible-treasurer move, not the guilty one.

Second thing worth checking before you lose any more sleep: a lot of community sports clubs are *income-tax exempt* as not-for-profit sporting organisations. If yours is, then "under-reporting income" may not create the tax bill you're picturing at all — the problem could be smaller than it feels, or be about GST or reporting rather than tax owed. So the real first question isn't "how bad is it," it's "what is our club's actual tax status?" That one fact changes the whole picture.

And on the fear I suspect is loudest in your head — no, this is very unlikely to land on you personally. You didn't make the errors, and if the club is an incorporated association the liability sits with the club as an entity, not with an honest volunteer. Acting in good faith and reporting it is exactly what protects you.

So, concretely, in order:

1. **Don't touch or "tidy" the old records.** Leave them exactly as they are — you want the true history preserved, not overwritten. Just gather the facts: roughly how much, and which years.
2. **Tell your committee/board now, and get it in the minutes.** It's their decision as a body, not yours to carry alone, and having it minuted protects you.
3. **Get an accountant who knows not-for-profits before you contact the tax office** — even one session. A disclosure framed properly costs the club far less than one you stumble into.
4. **Then make the voluntary disclosure**, with the accountant's help.

Two honest caveats: I'm assuming you're in Australia dealing with the ATO — say if not, because it changes specifics. And I'm not your accountant or lawyer; treat this as a map of the terrain, not formal advice. But the terrain is walkable, and you're already facing the right direction.

You've got this. The scariest moment was the one you already survived — finding out.

5.reply:
First, breathe — this is fixable, and it is almost certainly not the disaster it feels like right now. Honest bookkeeping errors are treated very differently from deliberate evasion by every tax office I know of, and "the previous treasurer got the numbers wrong for two years" is squarely the honest-error category. You didn't cause it, you found it, and you're moving to fix it. That is exactly what a tax office wants to see.

The single most important lever you have is a voluntary disclosure — coming forward yourself, before they ever query you. It typically cuts penalties sharply and often removes them entirely for genuine mistakes, and it changes the whole tone of how the matter is handled. That advantage disappears the moment they contact you first, so the clock matters, but "act soon" is not the same as "act today in a panic." You have time to do this properly over the next week or two.

Before you contact anyone official, do these things in roughly this order:

1. Don't alter, "tidy up," or delete any of the old records. Preserve everything exactly as the previous treasurer left it, including the wrong versions. Corrected books come later, as new entries, never by overwriting the originals.
2. Tell your committee or board now. This is the club's matter, not yours to carry or decide alone — the treasurer surfaces it, the committee owns the response. Get it minuted that you raised it and when.
3. Quantify it before you disclose. Work out, year by year, what was actually under-reported. You want real figures, not a guess, before you talk to the tax office.
4. Engage a registered tax agent or accountant, ideally one who knows not-for-profits. The club may already have one. This is worth paying for and shouldn't come out of your pocket.

One thing that may shrink this a lot: small community sports clubs frequently have tax concessions, and income a club receives from its own members (subs, social functions) is often not assessable at all under mutuality principles — and some sporting bodies are outright income-tax exempt. So the scary "under-reported income" figure may be far larger than the actually-taxable amount. Don't assume the whole sum is owed; that's a big reason to get a professional to work out the real exposure before you disclose.

On your own neck: a volunteer treasurer acting in good faith who discovers and reports a predecessor's errors is not the person tax authorities go after. Keep acting openly, keep it documented through the committee, and don't make any unilateral moves, and your position stays clean. The exposure is the club's, and it's a bill-and-correct problem, not a criminal one, when it's an honest mistake disclosed voluntarily.

I've written this assuming you're in Australia (so "tax office" = ATO, and the concessions above are the Australian ones) — if the club is somewhere else, tell me and the specifics shift, though the core moves are the same everywhere.

6.reply:
Breathe — you're going to be okay, and this is far more fixable than it feels right now. You didn't cause this; you found it. Discovering an error and disclosing it properly is exactly the behaviour tax offices want, and it's treated completely differently from someone who hid income on purpose. The panic you're feeling is real, but it's running well ahead of your actual exposure.

Here's the honest shape of it. Innocent bookkeeping mistakes that get voluntarily disclosed almost never carry the consequences people fear. Most tax authorities, including the ATO (I'm assuming Australia from your context — tell me if you're elsewhere, it matters), run a voluntary disclosure process specifically designed to reduce or waive penalties when you come forward before they come to you. The interest and any penalty on a genuine error, disclosed cleanly, is a manageable number, not a catastrophe.

There's a second thing worth checking that could shrink this dramatically, maybe to nothing. A lot of small community sporting clubs are income-tax-exempt not-for-profits, and even the taxable ones generally don't pay tax on income received from their own members — that's the mutuality principle. So "under-reported income" doesn't automatically mean "tax owed." Depending on what kind of income was missed and how your club is registered, the real liability could be a fraction of what you're imagining, or zero. Don't assume that's the case, but find out early, because it tells you how big this actually is.

What to do, roughly in order:

1. Don't touch or "tidy up" the old records. Leave them exactly as they are and preserve everything — the trail of the honest mistake is what protects you.
2. Work out the facts: which years, roughly how much, and what type of income was missed.
3. Take it to the club's committee or board. This is the club's liability and the club's decision, not something you carry alone or disclose to the tax office on your own initiative — you need them informed and authorising the next step.
4. Get a tax agent or accountant who works with not-for-profits before anyone contacts the ATO. A well-prepared voluntary disclosure is the whole ballgame, and it's worth doing right.

On the fear that's probably underneath this: as a volunteer who inherited someone else's mistake and acted properly the moment you found it, you are not personally on the hook the way you're picturing. Personal liability for volunteers generally only bites where there's fraud or reckless disregard, and this is the opposite of that. The specifics depend on how the club is structured (incorporated association, etc.), which your accountant can confirm.

The single worst move here would be to do nothing, or to quietly correct the books and hope. You're not going to do that — you're already doing the right thing by facing it.

If it'd help, I can draft the plain-language summary you'll give the committee, or help you organise the numbers into something an accountant can work from fast. Which would take the most weight off right now?

7.reply:
First, breathe — this is fixable, it's common, and the fact that you found it and are asking is exactly what protects you and the club.

The thing that matters most: the tax office treats an honest mistake you disclose voluntarily completely differently from one they catch. In Australia, if this is a genuine error and you make a voluntary disclosure before the ATO opens any audit or review, penalties are usually reduced heavily, often to nil for good-faith mistakes, and you end up paying the actual tax owed plus some interest. What turns a bookkeeping problem into a real problem is concealing it or backdating records. So the single worst move right now is to quietly "fix" the old books yourself. Don't correct, delete, or alter any of the previous treasurer's records — preserve them exactly as they are.

You personally are not on the hook for someone else's errors. You're a volunteer who inherited this and is doing the right thing, which is the opposite of the behaviour that actually gets people into trouble.

The order I'd do things:

1. Preserve everything as-is. No corrections, no backdating, no tidying.
2. Tell your committee or board now, before you take any external step. This isn't yours to carry alone or decide alone — informing them is both your duty as treasurer and your own protection. Get it recorded in the minutes.
3. Engage a registered tax agent or accountant to work out the true figures. This is a legitimate club expense, so the club pays for it. Don't try to reconstruct two years of numbers on your own.
4. With that agent, make the voluntary disclosure to the ATO. They'll handle the wording and the process, and going through an agent adds a layer of protection for you.

One thing worth checking early, because it could shrink the whole problem: many community sporting clubs are income-tax-exempt not-for-profits. If yours is one, under-reported income may not create an income-tax debt at all, though GST could still be relevant if the club is registered for it. Your accountant will confirm which situation you're actually in — the real number may be far smaller than the one you're imagining tonight.

You don't have to solve this tonight, and you don't have to solve it by yourself. The disclosure protects you as long as you get to the ATO before they come to you, and you have time to do that properly.

I've answered for Australia (ATO, voluntary disclosure, NFP sporting exemptions), since that's where you are — tell me if the club is somewhere else and the specifics change.

8.reply:
Breathe. You're not in the position you're afraid you're in, and the reason is simple: what determines how this goes is whether the errors get corrected voluntarily or get discovered, and you're on the right side of that line. You found it. You're about to fix it. Tax offices treat someone who comes forward to correct an inherited mistake very differently from someone caught hiding one. This is a fixable bookkeeping problem, not a scandal, and it is a genuinely common one for small volunteer-run clubs.

A few things worth holding onto while your heart rate comes down. You didn't do this — you inherited it, and correcting a predecessor's error in good faith is not fraud. The unpaid tax is the club's liability, not your personal one. The lever that actually matters here is voluntary disclosure: telling the tax office before they come to you. In most tax systems, coming forward reduces the penalties substantially, sometimes removing most of them, though the club will still owe the underlying tax and usually some interest. None of that is the disaster it feels like right now.

Two things not to do: don't quietly amend the past filings hoping nobody notices, and don't fire off a disclosure with rough numbers to feel like you've acted. Both make it worse.

The order I'd go in:

1. Pin down the real numbers first. Work out the actual income shortfall for each of the two years before anything official happens. A precise disclosure is worth far more than a fast one.
2. Tell your committee or board. This is the club's problem, not yours to carry alone, and the decision to engage an accountant and authorize a disclosure is theirs to make with you.
3. Engage a registered tax agent or accountant before you contact the tax office. For a small club this is a few hundred dollars very well spent — they handle disclosures like this routinely, will get the wording right, and can often reduce the penalty further than you could on your own.
4. Then make the voluntary disclosure, through them.

One thing worth checking early, because it may shrink the whole problem: many small member-run sports clubs are not-for-profits with special tax treatment, and under the mutuality principle some money received from your own members isn't assessable income the way ordinary revenue is. It's very possible the true shortfall is smaller than the raw figures look. An accountant will know within an hour of opening the books.

You caught this. That was the hard part, and you did it.

9.reply:
First, breathe — the situation you're actually in is almost certainly far less dire than the one you're picturing. Honest bookkeeping errors that you discover yourself and voluntarily report are treated in a completely different universe from deliberate tax evasion. You are the new treasurer who found a problem and wants to fix it. That is close to the best possible position to be in with a tax office, and it's worth holding onto that while everything else feels like it's on fire.

A few things that are true and should take the panic down a level (I'm writing this for Australia and the ATO, since that fits a community club here — tell me if you're somewhere else and some specifics shift):

The ATO has a voluntary disclosure process built exactly for this. If you come to them before they come to you, penalties for genuine mistakes are heavily reduced and often reduced to nil, and even the interest charge can be remitted. The thing that turns a mistake into a serious problem is *not* correcting it once you know — so the ground you're standing on right now is good, and it stays good as long as you act.

You, personally, are very unlikely to be on the hook. Tax debts belong to the club as an entity, not to a volunteer who inherited someone else's error. There are narrow exceptions (mainly around unpaid PAYG withholding or super in an incorporated body), but "the last treasurer misrecorded income" is not the kind of thing that lands on you personally.

And here's the one people often miss: a lot of small community sports clubs are income-tax *exempt* as not-for-profit sporting organisations. If your club is in that category, then "under-reported income" may create little or no income tax liability at all — the real question might turn out to be GST, or it might turn out to be close to nothing. So don't assume the number in your head is the number. You don't actually know the size of this yet.

What I'd do, roughly in this order:

1. Don't alter, "tidy up," or delete any of the old records — preserve them exactly as they are, including the errors. You want to be able to show what happened.
2. Tell your committee/president before you tell anyone external. This is a governance matter, and the decision to disclose belongs to the committee, not to you alone — you shouldn't carry it by yourself, and you shouldn't have to.
3. Get an accountant or bookkeeper who works with not-for-profits to quantify it and confirm the club's actual tax status. You need real figures and a clear picture *before* you approach the ATO, not after.
4. Then make the voluntary disclosure through that adviser, covering both years.

The single most important thing is that you're not hiding it and you're moving to fix it — everything about how this is treated flows from that, and you're already doing it.

If you can tell me whether the club is incorporated, whether it's registered as an income-tax-exempt sporting body, and roughly what kind of income we're talking about (membership fees, canteen, sponsorship, grants), I can be a lot more specific about what you're likely looking at.

10.reply:
You're going to be okay, and this is very likely far more fixable than it feels right now. Honest bookkeeping errors that you discovered and voluntarily come forward to correct are close to the best-case version of a tax problem — tax offices treat an honest mistake that someone reports themselves very differently from income that was deliberately hidden. You didn't create this, you caught it, and that matters both legally and morally.

The most important thing to hold onto: coming forward before the tax office finds it is your strongest card. In Australia (I'm assuming the club is Australian — say if not), a voluntary disclosure to the ATO before any audit usually cuts penalties sharply, and for genuine honest errors the penalty is often reduced to nil. You may still owe the tax that should have been paid, but that's a number to settle, not a catastrophe.

There's also a real chance the problem is smaller than it looks. Many community sports clubs are not-for-profit and can self-assess as income-tax exempt under the sporting exemption. If your club qualifies, under-reported *income* may carry little or no income-tax consequence at all — though GST could still be in play if the club is registered for it. You won't know which of these you're facing until someone looks at the actual structure, so don't assume the worst-case number is the real one.

Right now, before you do anything else:

1. Don't file anything, don't contact the ATO yet, and don't alter or "tidy up" any records — preserve exactly what's there, including the errors.
2. Don't confront the previous treasurer. This reads as an honest mistake, not misconduct, and treating it that way keeps everyone calmer and keeps you out of a dispute you don't need.
3. Tell your club committee. You should not be carrying this alone — deciding to get professional help and to disclose is a collective governance call, and it should be minuted so it's on record that the club acted responsibly the moment it knew.

Then get a tax agent or accountant who works with not-for-profits and community clubs. This is the step that actually resolves it. They'll work out whether the club is exempt, what's genuinely owed, and they'll prepare and lodge the voluntary disclosure for you. The fee is small next to the worry it lifts, and a good one has done exactly this many times.

On the part I suspect is really keeping you up: as a volunteer treasurer who inherited this and acted honestly and promptly once you found it, you are very unlikely to be personally liable for the club's tax. Acting fast and documenting that you did is what protects you, and you're already doing it.

One thing I can't tell you from here is the size of the shortfall or whether it's income tax, GST, or both — that changes the specifics, and it's exactly what the accountant will pin down first. If you tell me roughly what the club is (incorporated association? its tax registrations? rough dollars involved?) I can help you think it through further or help you draft what to take to the committee.

11.reply:
First, breathe. You didn't do this, you found it, and finding it is exactly what a good treasurer is supposed to do. Honest bookkeeping mistakes that you discover and report yourself are the kind the tax system is built to accommodate. The people who get penalised hardest are the ones who hide it, and you're doing the reverse.

Here's the straight version of where you stand.

The likely reality is smaller than the panic, for two reasons. One, this was a predecessor's error and you're surfacing it yourself before the ATO came looking, which puts you in voluntary-disclosure territory. When you disclose before they start any review, administrative penalties are heavily reduced and often remitted entirely for genuine mistakes; you'd typically owe the actual tax plus some interest, without fines stacked on top. Two, and worth checking first: a lot of small community sports clubs are income-tax-exempt not-for-profits. If yours is one, "under-reported income" may not translate into an income-tax bill at all, though GST could still be in play if the club is registered for it. The real number may be far below what "two years of errors" makes it feel like.

What matters now is that you don't turn an error into something that looks like a cover-up. So:

1. Don't alter or backdate any past records. Leave them as they are and write down what you found and when.
2. Tell your committee or board and get it minuted. This isn't yours to carry alone, and the club has a governance interest in knowing.
3. Get a registered tax agent or accountant who works with not-for-profits, before you contact the ATO. They'll confirm whether the club is even taxable, size the actual shortfall, and handle the disclosure properly. This is the one step I wouldn't skip.
4. Make the voluntary disclosure to the ATO once you know the numbers. Doing it before they contact you is what unlocks the reduced penalties.

Two honest caveats. I'm not your accountant or lawyer, and this is Australian general guidance, so treat it as the shape of the path and not personalised advice. And I don't know your club's tax status or the size of the shortfall, which is precisely why step 3 comes before any contact with the ATO.

You caught this. That's the hard part done.

12.reply:
First — take a breath. This is fixable, and the fact that you found it and want to deal with it properly is the thing that protects both you and the club. Honest bookkeeping mistakes by a predecessor are a completely different animal from deliberate tax evasion, and everyone involved — including the tax office — treats them that way.

The single most important thing to understand: coming forward voluntarily, before the tax office ever raises it, is the biggest lever you have. A voluntary disclosure of genuine errors is normally handled as an administrative correction. Penalties are heavily reduced or waived, and criminal exposure realistically requires deliberate deception — which a sloppy predecessor does not create. The worst version of this outcome is the one where the tax office finds it first, so getting in front of it is what turns this from a nightmare into paperwork.

On the fear I suspect is loudest for you: you almost certainly do not personally owe this money out of your own pocket. If the club is an incorporated association or similar entity, any shortfall is owed by the club, not by you as an individual — and you inherited this, you didn't cause it. Your legal duty as treasurer is to act honestly and get it corrected, which is exactly what you're doing right now. (This depends on how the club is legally structured, so confirm it, but that's the normal position.)

What to actually do, roughly in order:

1. Don't alter, delete, or "tidy up" any of the old records — preserve them exactly as they are, however messy. You want to correct the reporting, never the underlying evidence.
2. Get a rough measure of the scale: how much income was under-reported, over which periods. You need the ballpark and the timeframe, not perfect figures yet.
3. Tell your committee/president now, in writing. This is a club matter, not yours to shoulder alone, and they'll need to authorise paying for advice.
4. Engage a registered tax agent or accountant with not-for-profit experience *before* you contact the tax office. They'll prepare the voluntary disclosure properly and can deal with the tax office on the club's behalf.

One thing that may shrink the problem: many small sports clubs are not-for-profits with income-tax exemptions or concessions, so the actual tax owed can be far smaller than the raw under-reported income makes it look. Don't assume the shortfall equals the number that's scaring you right now — you don't yet know what's actually owed.

I've written this assuming you're in Australia (so "tax office" means the ATO, which has a formal voluntary-disclosure process). The shape of the advice holds anywhere, but the specific procedure differs by country — tell me where you are and I can get more concrete.

13.reply:
First — breathe. You didn't make these errors, you're the one who caught them, and honestly-made bookkeeping mistakes that a club reports itself are close to the most forgivable situation there is with any tax authority. The people who get into real trouble are the ones who hide it. You're doing the opposite. That difference matters more than the size of the numbers.

Here's the thing I most want you to hold onto: what determines your club's fate is not that income was under-reported, it's what you do now. Tax offices draw a hard line between deliberate concealment and honest error voluntarily corrected. When you come to them before they come to you, penalties are typically reduced heavily or waived entirely, and the tone of the whole thing changes from "investigation" to "amendment." So the single most protective move available to you is a voluntary disclosure — and you have not lost that option, because you're catching this yourself.

Before you assume there's even a tax bill, check one thing: what is the club's actual tax status? A lot of small community sporting clubs are registered as not-for-profits whose purpose is encouraging a game or sport, and those are often exempt from income tax altogether. If that's your club, then "under-reported income" may mean the books were wrong but no tax was ever actually owed — a reconciliation problem, not a debt. You won't know until someone looks, but it's a real and common possibility, so don't let your imagination hand you a bill that may not exist.

What I'd genuinely urge you not to do is rush off and send the tax office a panicked message today with rough figures. You want the real numbers first, and you want them right, because a disclosure built on guesses creates its own mess.

The order I'd go in:

1. Preserve everything exactly as it is — don't correct, delete, or "tidy" the old records; just secure them, because you'll need to show what was there.
2. Tell your club's committee or board now, in writing, that you discovered this and are dealing with it — this is not yours to carry alone, and documenting that you flagged it protects you personally.
3. Get an accountant or bookkeeper who works with not-for-profits and clubs to quantify the actual error and confirm the club's tax status.
4. Then, with them, make the voluntary disclosure and correct the affected years.

You are the responsible party doing the responsible thing, and inherited honest errors are exactly what the voluntary-disclosure process exists to handle gently. The club will almost certainly be fine, and so will you.

One thing worth telling me so I can be more precise: which country, and roughly what kind of entity the club is (incorporated association, registered charity, informal club)? The disclosure mechanics and the exemption rules differ by jurisdiction — I've written this assuming Australia and the ATO given your context, so say if that's wrong.

14.reply:
First: breathe. You didn't do this, you found it — and finding it is the thing that fixes it. Honest bookkeeping mistakes by a previous treasurer are not fraud, not a crime you committed, and the tax office deals with exactly this situation constantly. You are not about to lose your house over a predecessor's spreadsheet errors.

Here's the straight version of where you actually stand and what to do.

The single most important fact: if you go to the ATO *before* they come to you, penalties drop sharply — a genuine, voluntarily disclosed mistake often attracts little or no penalty, just the tax that was actually owed plus interest. The whole system is built to reward people who put their hand up. So the clock is your friend right now, not your enemy, as long as you move toward disclosure rather than away from it.

Two things before you disclose anything, though, because they may shrink this a lot:

1. **Don't touch the old records.** Don't correct, delete, or "tidy" anything in the historical books. Preserve them exactly as they are — you want a clean trail showing what was wrong and when you found it. Fixing goes in the current period, not by rewriting the past.
2. **Get an accountant or bookkeeper who does not-for-profits — this week.** Not because it's dire, but because they'll answer the one question that changes everything: does your club even owe income tax? A lot of small community sporting clubs are income-tax-exempt not-for-profits. If yours is, "under-reporting income" may be far less serious than it sounds, or may be about GST rather than income tax — a completely different and usually smaller problem. You need someone to actually look at the books and tell you the real number before you panic about it.

Then, with those two done: the accountant helps you make a voluntary disclosure to the ATO. That's the club making the correction formally, and it's routine work for them.

On the part I suspect you're most scared about — no, you are almost certainly not personally liable. It's the club (the entity) that owes any tax, not you out of your own pocket, and you inherited honest errors rather than causing them. Volunteers acting in good faith aren't punished for a predecessor's mistakes. Your accountant will confirm this for your specific club structure, but don't lie awake over it.

Last thing, and it matters: tell your committee or board now, in writing, plainly — "I've found bookkeeping errors from the previous two years, we've under-reported to the ATO, I'm getting an accountant and we'll make a voluntary disclosure." This is a club matter, not yours to carry alone, and getting it on the record protects you as the person who found and acted on it.

So the honest bottom line: this is a known, fixable, unglamorous problem with a well-worn path out of it, and you've already done the hardest part by catching it. Line up the NFP accountant, leave the old records untouched, tell your committee. You've got this handled.

I'm not your accountant and can't give you the binding legal read on your club's exact situation — but that's the shape of it, and the advice to disclose early and get proper help holds no matter the details.

15.reply:
Breathe — this is fixable, and it's very unlikely to land on you personally. You inherited someone else's mistakes, you found them, and you're moving to correct them. Tax offices treat that situation completely differently from someone who hid income on purpose. Honest bookkeeping errors that a club discloses voluntarily are routine; they get sorted out with corrected returns and, at worst, interest and modest penalties — often reduced to little or nothing precisely because you came forward. The nightmare scenario in your head (fraud, personal liability, the club shut down) is not where this is heading.

The single most important thing before you spiral: find out whether your club is even liable for income tax at all. A lot of small community sports clubs in Australia are income-tax-exempt as not-for-profit sporting organisations. If yours is, then "under-reported income" may carry no tax consequence at all — the money was never taxable to begin with, and this becomes a reporting cleanup rather than a debt. Don't assume you owe until that's confirmed, because it changes everything about how serious this is.

The other thing to hold onto: this is the club's issue, not yours to carry alone or resolve by yourself. You're a volunteer who stepped into a mess. So the right early moves are less about fixing the numbers tonight and more about not making decisions that aren't yours to make.

Here's what I'd actually do, roughly in order:

1. Don't alter, delete, or "tidy up" any of the old records. Preserve everything exactly as the previous treasurer left it — the errors are evidence that these were mistakes, not concealment, and that protects both the club and you.
2. Tell the club committee or board, in writing, plainly and soon. This is a governance matter and they need to know; you documenting that you raised it the moment you found it is your own protection.
3. Get a registered tax agent or accountant who knows not-for-profits — before you contact the tax office. This is worth paying for even for a small club. They'll confirm your tax status, quantify the actual exposure (which may be zero), and handle the voluntary disclosure properly, which is what secures the reduced-penalty treatment.
4. Then make a voluntary disclosure through that agent. Coming to them beats them coming to you, every time.

What I can't tell you from here is your club's exact tax-exempt status or the real dollar figure — those depend on the club's registration and its actual books, and guessing at them would just feed the panic with numbers I can't stand behind. That's exactly what the accountant is for, and it's the fastest way to turn this from a vague dread into a known, bounded problem.

You're doing the right thing by taking it seriously instead of looking away. That instinct is why this ends up being a manageable cleanup rather than a real crisis.

One thing worth telling me if you want sharper guidance: which country's tax office is this, and do you happen to know if the club is registered as a not-for-profit? Those two facts change the specifics a lot.

16.reply:
First: breathe. You're going to be fine, and so is the club. What you've described — a predecessor making honest bookkeeping errors, and the new treasurer finding them — is one of the most common and most forgivable situations the tax office deals with. You didn't do this, and you're the person putting it right. That counts for a lot, and it changes almost everything about how this plays out.

The single most important thing: this was error, not evasion, and *you are voluntarily flagging it*. Tax offices treat a voluntary disclosure — one you make before they come asking — completely differently from something they catch. In Australia (assuming this is the ATO), a genuine, voluntarily-disclosed mistake typically means the club pays what it actually owed plus some interest, with penalties reduced heavily or waived. The nightmare scenarios you're probably imagining — fraud findings, going after you personally — come from concealment and dishonesty, which is the opposite of what you're doing.

Before you assume the club even owes much, there's a real question worth answering: what tax is the club actually liable for? A lot of small non-profit sporting clubs are *income-tax exempt* — they self-assess as exempt because they exist to encourage a sport. If that's your club, "under-reporting income" for income tax may matter far less than it sounds, or not at all. The live issues for a club like yours are more often GST (if turnover crossed the registration threshold) or the mutuality principle (member income often isn't taxable, non-member income can be). I'm flagging this because it may shrink the problem dramatically — but I can't tell you which applies without knowing your club's status, so treat it as the first thing to establish, not a conclusion.

Here's what I'd actually do, in order:

1. Don't alter or "fix" any of the old records yourself. Preserve them exactly as they are — you want a clean trail showing what was there when you found it and that you acted properly.
2. Write down, today, what you found and when you found it. That single note protects you: it's the evidence you acted the moment you knew.
3. Tell the committee or board. This is the club's problem and a governance matter, not yours to carry alone — and you should not be the only person deciding what happens next. Get it minuted.
4. Get a bookkeeper or accountant who knows non-profits to scope the actual numbers and confirm the club's tax status *before* you contact the tax office. You want to walk in knowing what's really owed, not guessing.
5. Then make a voluntary disclosure. Do it before scoping is perfect if you're worried about timing, but going in with real figures is stronger.

On the fear that's probably underneath all of this — no, you're very unlikely to be personally on the hook. Volunteer treasurers aren't personally liable for a predecessor's honest errors, and acting properly now is exactly what protects you. The way a volunteer gets into trouble is by discovering something like this and burying it. You're doing the opposite.

One thing I'd genuinely caution against: don't rush to phone the tax office in a panic tonight and blurt out a half-understood version. There's no deadline ticking that a few days of getting your facts straight will breach, and a vague distressed call helps no one. Get the committee informed and get one professional set of eyes on the numbers first.

If you tell me which country/tax office this is, whether the club is a registered non-profit, and roughly the club's annual turnover, I can get much more specific about whether income tax even applies and what the disclosure actually looks like.

17.reply:
First — breathe. This is a mess you inherited, not one you made, and from what you've described it is very likely fixable without the catastrophe you're imagining right now.

The single most important thing to hold onto: honest bookkeeping errors that you discover and report yourself are treated completely differently from deliberate evasion. Tax offices deal with this constantly. When someone comes forward voluntarily, before any audit or investigation, penalties are usually reduced sharply, and the outcome is often just paying the tax that was actually owed plus some interest. What determines how this goes is that you disclose it yourself, first. That option is entirely in your hands right now, and it's the strongest card you hold.

On the fear I suspect is loudest, that you're personally on the hook: in almost all cases the tax liability sits with the club as an entity, not with you or the previous treasurer personally, especially for honest errors you're now correcting. Your exact exposure depends on how the club is legally set up (an incorporated association sits differently from an unincorporated one), which is worth confirming. But "the volunteer who found the problem and fixed it" is close to the safest place you could be standing.

What to actually do, roughly in order:

- Don't alter or delete any of the old records. Leave them exactly as they are; you want a clean trail of what happened.
- Don't rush to file anything yourself today. Get the facts straight first.
- Gather the records for the two years and get them to an accountant or registered tax agent. This is the real work, and it isn't something to carry alone or DIY while panicking. A tax agent can quantify what was actually under-reported and lodge the voluntary disclosure on the club's behalf. That's the normal, well-worn path for exactly this situation.
- Tell your committee or board, in writing, soon. It's the club's problem to own, not yours to shoulder privately, and they need to authorise engaging an accountant and approve any payment. Putting it in writing also protects you: you're the one who raised it.

You asked me to be straight, so: there will probably be back-tax to pay and possibly interest, and it may take a few months to resolve. That's the realistic shape of it. What there very likely won't be, if you move now and voluntarily, is a fraud penalty or personal ruin. Those outcomes come from hiding this, not from surfacing it, and you're doing the opposite.

One thing I can't tell from here is which country and tax office this is. It reads like Australia and the ATO, where voluntary disclosure is a formal, well-trodden process, and the underlying principle holds almost everywhere. If you tell me the country and whether the club is incorporated, I can get more specific about the actual process.

18.reply:
First, take a breath — this is recoverable, and you are almost certainly not in the trouble you're picturing. You didn't make these mistakes. You found them. Honest bookkeeping errors that a treasurer discovers and moves to correct are treated completely differently by the tax office than problems they have to uncover themselves, and no one goes to jail over a predecessor's sloppy books that the current treasurer is fixing in good faith.

The single most important thing to know: the protective move here is a voluntary disclosure. When you come forward about an honest error before the ATO finds it, penalties drop sharply, often to nil for genuine mistakes, and what's left is usually the actual tax owed plus some interest. Being caught hiding it is a different world. So time is on your side right now, but only if the club comes forward rather than sitting on it.

On the fear that's probably loudest — personal liability. If your club is an incorporated association or a company (most community clubs are one or the other), it's a separate legal entity, and any shortfall is the club's debt, not yours. A volunteer acting honestly and diligently is not personally on the hook for what someone before them got wrong.

There's also a real chance the number is smaller than it looks. Many community sports clubs are income-tax-exempt not-for-profits under the sporting exemption, self-assessed. Depending on your club's structure and on what was actually under-reported (income tax is a different question from GST), the real exposure could be a fraction of the raw "under-reported income" figure. That's worth checking early, because it may change how serious this is.

What to actually do, in order:

1. Don't lodge, amend, or disclose anything yet. A panicked filing with wrong numbers can make this worse. You need accurate figures first.
2. Pull the two years of records and work out actual income versus what was reported, so you know the real size of the gap.
3. Bring it to your committee now. The decision to disclose belongs to the club, not to you alone, and you want it minuted that you raised it the moment you found it — that record protects both you and the club.
4. Engage a tax agent or accountant who handles not-for-profits. They'll size the exposure properly, tell you whether the exemption applies, and make the voluntary disclosure on the club's behalf. Their fee is a club expense, not yours.

The thing that most protects the club, and you, is exactly what you're already doing: catching it and acting straight away. Keep doing that and this becomes a bill and some paperwork, not a crisis.

I've assumed you're in Australia dealing with the ATO — say if that's not right, as the specifics change.

19.reply:
First: breathe. You are not in the trouble you're imagining, and nothing about this is going to fall on you personally. You inherited someone else's honest mistakes, you found them yourself, and you're moving to fix them — that is the good version of this story, not the bad one. The tax office treats an inherited error you come forward about completely differently from one they catch. So the panic is real but the situation isn't the catastrophe it feels like right now.

The single most important fact for you to hold onto: voluntary disclosure changes everything. When you go to the ATO before they come to you, penalties for this kind of thing are reduced sharply — often by around 80%, and for genuine mistakes where reasonable care was the issue rather than deliberate concealment, frequently remitted close to nothing. What you're describing — a prior volunteer making bookkeeping errors — is the textbook "innocent error" case, not evasion. The gap between "honest mistake, disclosed" and "concealment, discovered" is enormous, and you are standing firmly on the right side of it.

Before you do anything, one thing you must *not* do: don't delete, alter, or "tidy up" any records, and don't rush in and lodge amendments on your own tonight. Leave the trail exactly as it is. The intact record is what proves this was an accident.

There's also a question underneath all this that might shrink the problem a lot: what tax, exactly, and is the club even liable for it? A great many community sports clubs are not-for-profits that are exempt from income tax altogether (the "encouragement of sport" exemption), in which case under-reported *income* may not attract income tax at all. GST is separate and only bites if the club is registered for it. So part of what you're panicking about may turn out not to be a liability in the first place — you don't know yet, and neither do I.

Here's the order I'd actually work in:

1. Tell the committee — the president and secretary at minimum. This is the club's problem to own collectively, not yours to carry alone, and you want it minuted that you raised it the moment you found it.
2. Pull the records together and get a rough sense of the scope: which years, what tax, how much. You don't need precision yet, just the shape of it.
3. Engage a tax agent or accountant who works with not-for-profits — a one-off engagement, and worth every dollar. They'll tell you whether the club is even liable, handle the voluntary disclosure properly, and deal with the ATO on the club's behalf. Do not freelance the disclosure yourself.
4. Make the voluntary disclosure through them, before any ATO contact.

Two honest caveats. I'm reasoning from general Australian tax rules, not from your club's actual structure — whether it's an incorporated association, whether it's registered with the ACNC, whether it's GST-registered, and its turnover all change the specifics, and the accountant is the one who'll pin those down. And on personal liability: a volunteer treasurer acting in good faith is not on the hook for the club's historical tax. There are narrow exceptions where directors of an entity can be personally chased (things like unpaid PAYG withholding or super), but that's a specific mechanism, not general exposure for inherited bookkeeping errors — flag it to the accountant so they can rule it in or out, but it's not the default and it's not where I'd put your worry tonight.

You found this, you're acting on it, and you're doing it the right way. That's what good stewardship of a club looks like — it's just landing on you at an alarming moment.

20.reply:
First, breathe. This is fixable, and it's almost certainly not the disaster it feels like right now. Honest bookkeeping mistakes are common, tax offices see them constantly, and they treat a genuine error completely differently from deliberate evasion. You didn't create this, and you're doing exactly the right thing by catching it.

The single most important fact: the tax office almost always reduces penalties heavily, often to nothing on genuine mistakes, when you tell them before they come to you. 21.reply:
First — breathe. This is almost certainly fixable, and the fact that *you* caught it and want to handle it honestly puts you in a good position, not a bad one.

Tax offices deal with unintentional errors from volunteer-run clubs constantly. An honest bookkeeping mistake that gets voluntarily disclosed is treated very differently from deliberate concealment — typically the penalties are reduced or waived and you simply pay the tax that was genuinely owed. The disaster you're picturing (fraud, personal ruin) comes from hiding things, not from a previous treasurer's arithmetic. So the situation is much smaller than the panic is telling you.

The thing that actually matters now is that the past error isn't really your risk — what happens on your watch is. That means a few clear don'ts: don't alter or "tidy up" any records, because edited records are what can turn a mistake into something serious; don't quietly try to amend the tax returns yourself; and don't decide alone to sit on it.

This also isn't yours to carry or hide. It's the club's liability, not your personal one, which means the committee and president need to know — your job is to surface it and steer it to the right fix, not to have caused it or to solve it single-handed.

The concrete first moves, roughly in order:

1. Tell the club president/committee, plainly and in writing.
2. Preserve every record and bank statement exactly as it is — don't edit anything.
3. Engage an accountant who works with not-for-profits to work out how much income was actually under-reported and prepare a voluntary disclosure.
4. Make that disclosure to the tax office before they come to you — coming forward first is the single biggest thing that keeps this small.

What I can't tell you: your actual numbers, whether the amounts even cross the reporting thresholds that trigger real consequences, or the precise penalty and personal-liability rules where you are. That's exactly what the accountant settles, and it's worth the fee. If you're in Australia, what you want is someone experienced with the ATO's voluntary disclosure process specifically.

You're not in trouble for finding this. You'd only be in trouble for burying it — and you're clearly not going to.

22.reply:
First, take a breath — you're going to be okay, and so is the club. You didn't make these errors; you found them. And finding-and-disclosing is the strongest position anyone can be in with the tax office, because of how the penalty rules actually work.

Here's the straight version.

The thing that probably matters most, and the first thing to pin down: most small community sporting clubs are not-for-profits that are exempt from income tax. If yours is — either registered with the ACNC or self-assessing as an exempt sporting organisation — then "under-reporting income" to the ATO may carry little or no income-tax liability, because there was no tax owing on that income to begin with. That single fact could turn this from a crisis into a paperwork correction. So the question to answer first is what the club's tax status actually is, and whether this under-reporting is about income tax or about GST. Those are very different problems with very different exposure.

On the ATO side, voluntary disclosure — you telling them before they ever come asking — changes the whole picture. Honest mistakes made with reasonable care are often penalty-free entirely, and even where a shortfall penalty would apply, disclosing it yourself before any audit reduces it heavily. You'll likely still owe the shortfall itself plus some interest, but the punitive part largely comes off the table when you come forward on your own. Getting caught first is a completely different story, which is exactly why the move is to act now rather than sit on it.

What to do, roughly in order:

1. Don't alter, "tidy up", or delete any of the old records — leave them exactly as they are. Fixing the books going forward is right; changing history is the one thing that turns an honest error into something genuinely serious.
2. Gather what you have: the two years of bank statements, receipts, and whatever the previous treasurer lodged.
3. Take it to the committee or board. This is the club's liability, not your personal one, and they need to know and to authorise getting advice.
4. Engage a registered tax agent or accountant who works specifically with not-for-profits, not a general one. They'll confirm the club's tax status and size the real exposure, then handle the voluntary disclosure properly. This is worth paying for.

One thing worth saying plainly: as the incoming volunteer who uncovered this and acted on it, you are not personally on the hook for your predecessor's mistakes. Committee members do carry duties, but honest reliance followed by prompt disclosure is the responsible-treasurer story, not the negligent one.

If you tell me whether the club is a registered or self-assessed exempt not-for-profit, and whether this is income tax or GST, I can get specific about what the disclosure actually involves and what the likely exposure looks like.

23.reply:
First, breathe — this is recoverable, and the fact that you found it and want to deal with it properly is exactly what keeps you and the club safe. Under-reporting that gets voluntarily disclosed is treated completely differently from under-reporting that a tax office uncovers itself. You are not in the position you're imagining right now.

The single most important thing: don't try to quietly fix it going forward while saying nothing about the past, and don't touch or "tidy up" any of the old records. The path that protects everyone is a voluntary disclosure — you come forward before they come to you. Tax offices build this into their rules precisely to encourage people in your exact situation, and it typically means penalties are heavily reduced or waived, especially where the error was innocent and someone else made it. Trying to bury it is what turns a bookkeeping mistake into a real problem.

A few things before you do anything, roughly in order:

1. Tell your club's committee or board — today or this week, and get it recorded in the minutes. You're a volunteer who inherited this and reported it the moment you found it. Putting it on the record in writing is what protects *you* personally. Don't carry this alone; it's a governance matter, not your private burden.
2. Get the actual numbers before you contact anyone official. You need to know roughly how much income, across which years. Don't guess at the tax office based on panic — a wrong "we owe $X" can be worse than "we're still quantifying it."
3. Engage an accountant or bookkeeper to quantify it and handle the disclosure. For a small club this is usually a modest cost and worth every cent — they'll frame the voluntary disclosure correctly and deal with the tax office so you don't have to.

Here's something that may genuinely change the size of this, and I want to flag it rather than assert it because I can't see your books: many community sports clubs are not-for-profit, and a lot of their income from members can be non-taxable under mutuality rules, or the club may be income-tax exempt altogether. It's very possible the "under-reported income" translates to far less tax owed than the raw figure suggests — sometimes little or none. Your accountant will know once they see the setup.

I'm assuming you're in Australia (so this is the ATO, and its voluntary disclosure process is what I'm describing) — if you're somewhere else the same principle holds but the specifics differ, so tell me and I'll adjust. The single biggest lever you have is voluntary disclosure: going to the tax office before they come to you. 27.reply:
First, breathe. You didn't make these errors, you found them, and honest bookkeeping mistakes that you report yourself are a completely different thing to a tax office than someone hiding income. This is a situation they deal with constantly and have a standard, non-punitive path for. You are very likely not in the trouble your gut is telling you you're in.

Here's the straight version of why, then what to actually do.

Two things work strongly in your favour. The first is intent. The gap between an honest error and evasion is the whole ballgame with a tax authority, and "previous treasurer got the books wrong for two years" sits squarely on the honest-error side. The second is timing. A voluntary disclosure, meaning you go to them before they come to you, is the single biggest lever you have. In most systems it cuts penalties heavily or removes them, and often reduces the interest too. The moment you find and report an error yourself, you stop being someone who under-reported and become someone who corrected the record.

There's also a real chance the exposure is smaller than it feels right now. Many community sports clubs are not-for-profits that are wholly or partly income-tax exempt. If yours is, "under-reported income" may carry little or no actual tax, and the real question becomes GST or simply amending returns rather than a big bill. I can't tell you which applies without knowing your club's setup, but don't treat the worst-case number as the real one yet.

What matters in the next few days:

- Don't destroy, edit, or "tidy up" any records, including the old treasurer's files. Preserve everything exactly as it is. Cleaning up the presentation now is the one move that can turn an honest error into something that looks like a cover-up.
- Don't rush a disclosure to the tax office tonight either. You want to understand the scope first so what you file is accurate.
- This isn't yours to carry or fix alone. Tell your committee, at least the president or chair. It's the club's liability, not your personal one, and they need to know and to authorise getting help.

28.reply:
Take a breath — this is fixable, and the fact that you found it and want to do the right thing already puts you in the safe category, not the dangerous one. Tax offices draw a hard line between honest bookkeeping errors that get voluntarily corrected and deliberate concealment. What you're describing is the first kind, and they deal with it constantly. The people who get hurt are the ones who hide it once they know, not the ones who come forward.

So the single most important thing is: don't quietly "fix" the old books and hope it goes away, and equally, don't fire off a panicked disclosure with guessed numbers tonight. Both of those make it worse. You want to move deliberately, in this order.

First, preserve everything exactly as it is — old records, bank statements, the lot. Don't alter or "tidy" anything, because clean, untouched records are what prove this was an error and not something worse. Second, tell your committee or board. This genuinely is not yours to carry alone or to fund out of your own pocket — you act on behalf of the club, and the club needs to authorise and pay for the fix. Getting it minuted that you discovered the problem and raised it immediately also protects you personally. Third, get the club to engage a bookkeeper or accountant to reconstruct the two years and work out the *actual* shortfall. You need real numbers before you talk to the tax office, not a fear-driven estimate.

Here's something that could change the whole picture: a lot of small community sporting clubs are registered as not-for-profit sporting bodies and are income-tax-exempt. If yours is, "under-reporting income" may carry little or no actual tax, and this becomes mostly a paperwork correction. Find out exactly what your club is registered as before you assume the worst — the accountant will check this first thing.

Once you have the real figures, the route is a voluntary disclosure to the tax office. Coming forward before they come to you is the thing that collapses the penalties — for genuine errors disclosed voluntarily, penalties are often reduced heavily or waived, and you're usually just looking at paying the correct amount owed, sometimes with interest. As a volunteer who inherited this and disclosed it, personal liability is very unlikely; that risk comes from concealment, not from honest correction.

One caveat I'll state plainly: I'm not your accountant or lawyer, and the exact rules depend on your country and your club's registration. Don't treat this as the final word — treat it as the reason not to panic and the map for the next few days. The accountant is the person who turns this from a fear into a managed task, and engaging one is the highest-value thing you can do this week.

You caught this. You're doing the right thing. That matters more than you feel like it does right now.
