29.reply:
First, breathe. This is recoverable, and you're closer to the best-case version of it than the panic is telling you. Inheriting honest bookkeeping errors from a predecessor and catching them yourself is about the most forgivable situation a tax office ever deals with. You didn't cause it, you found it, and coming forward voluntarily is exactly what turns it from a problem into a formality.

A few things that are true and worth holding onto. Tax authorities care enormously about the line between fraud and honest error — deliberate hiding of income sits in a completely different universe from a volunteer's mistakes in the books. Where errors are disclosed voluntarily, before the office comes asking, penalties are usually small or waived entirely, and the main cost is interest on whatever was actually owed, often not much beyond that. And you're almost certainly not personally on the hook: a volunteer treasurer acting in good faith isn't liable for a predecessor's mistakes. The club is the entity that owes anything owed, if it owes anything at all.

That last point matters more than it sounds, because one question could shrink this dramatically before you do anything else: is your club even liable for income tax? In Australia a great many community sports clubs are self-assessed as income-tax-exempt — non-profit bodies for the encouragement of a game or sport. If yours is, "under-reported income" may not translate into any income tax at all, and the real question becomes GST or activity-statement corrections, or simply setting the record straight. Establish this first. You may be bracing for something far larger than what's actually there.

What to actually do, in order:

1. Don't touch or "fix" the old records. Preserve them exactly as they are. You want a clean trail showing what happened and when you found it — quietly amending the past is the one move that can make an honest error start to look like a cover-up.
2. Tell your committee. This isn't yours to carry alone or to decide by yourself, and disclosing to the tax office is the club's call, not just the treasurer's. Note down when and how you discovered it.
3. Get an accountant or registered tax agent who works with non-profits, before contacting the tax office. They'll confirm the club's tax status, work out the real numbers, and frame the voluntary disclosure properly — how it's lodged genuinely affects the penalty outcome.
4. Make the disclosure through them. Coming forward before the office finds it is the single biggest lever in your favour, and it's still fully available to you.

The thing to move on this week is step 3, the accountant, because everything else — how big this really is, whether any tax is owed, how to word the disclosure — depends on their read. Two years is short, the sums in a small club are usually modest, and for a professional this is a well-worn path, not a crisis.

One assumption I've made: that you're in Australia (ATO). If you're somewhere else the specific exemptions and terminology differ, but the playbook is the same everywhere.

30.reply:
First, breathe — this is almost certainly fixable, and it's far less dangerous than it feels right now. The thing that actually matters to a tax office is the difference between honest mistakes and deliberate concealment, and what you have is the first kind: errors you didn't make, that you found yourself, that you're moving to correct. That is the good side of this line to be on.

A few things that are true and worth holding onto:

You didn't cause this, and catching it is the responsible act, not the guilty one. Treasurers inherit messes constantly; discovering one is how it gets fixed.

Honest errors voluntarily disclosed are treated very differently from errors the tax office has to chase down and catch. In Australia (which I'm assuming from your setup — say if not), the ATO has a formal voluntary-disclosure process, and coming forward before they find it typically means you pay the tax that was genuinely owed plus interest, with penalties heavily reduced or waived. The penalty regime is built to punish hiding, not honest correction.

You are very likely not personally on the hook. If the club is an incorporated association or similar, the liability sits with the *club* as an entity, not with you as an individual volunteer — and certainly not for a predecessor's honest mistakes. Personal exposure generally requires fraud or reckless conduct on *your* part, which is the opposite of what you're doing.

There may be less owed than you fear — possibly nothing in income tax. A lot of community sporting clubs are registered not-for-profits that are income-tax-exempt. If yours is, "under-reported income" may carry little or no income-tax consequence at all (though GST could still be in play if the club is GST-registered). Check the club's tax status before you assume the worst — it can change the whole picture.

Now, the practical part. In rough order:

1. Don't alter or "tidy up" the old records. Preserve exactly what you found — the errors are your evidence that this was a mistake, not concealment. Fixing the books quietly is the one move that could actually make this look bad.

2. Don't rush to phone the tax office or lodge amendments today. Understand the scope first — you can't disclose accurately what you haven't measured, and a panicked half-correct disclosure helps no one.

3. Tell the committee. This is the club's problem, not yours to carry alone, and the decision to disclose is theirs to make and minute. Getting it on the record also protects you personally.

4. Work out the numbers: how much, which years, and what kind of income was missed. Even a rough figure changes how urgent this is.

5. Get an accountant — ideally one who works with not-for-profits or sporting clubs. Voluntary disclosures are routine work for them; they'll know the exact process, whether the club was even liable, and how to frame it so penalties are minimised.

I can't give you the final legal answer on liability or the exact penalty position — that depends on the club's structure and your jurisdiction, and it's worth real advice. But the shape of this is a manageable cleanup handled by professionals who see it often, not a crisis you personally caused or personally owe. You're doing exactly the right thing by taking it seriously and moving carefully.

If you tell me the club's legal structure (incorporated association? tax-exempt NFP?), roughly how much income and which years, and whether it's GST-registered, I can help you think through the specific exposure and how to brief the committee.

First: you're not in the trouble you think you are. Honest bookkeeping errors that you found and are trying to fix are a completely different animal from fraud. Tax offices know the difference, and they treat people who come forward far better than people they catch. You inheriting a mess and reporting it is close to the best-case version of this story.

Here's what to actually do, roughly in order.

**Stop and preserve everything.** Don't correct, tidy, or "fix" any of the old records yet. Don't delete anything. Take the books exactly as they are. If they're paper or in a spreadsheet, make a copy and set the original aside untouched. You want a clean before-picture so nobody can later say the numbers were massaged.

**Write down what you found and when.** A short dated note to yourself: what the errors are, which years, how you noticed. This matters because it establishes that you disclosed voluntarily rather than got found out. That single fact usually drives whether penalties get reduced or waived.

**Don't try to size the whole thing alone.** You need to know three things before you talk to the tax office: which years are affected, how much income was under-reported each year, and whether the club actually owed tax on it. That last one is bigger than it sounds, keep reading.

**Check whether the club is even taxable.** A lot of small community sports clubs are income-tax-exempt as not-for-profits. If that's your club's status, under-reported income might create little or no actual tax debt, and this becomes a reporting cleanup rather than a "we owe years of back tax" problem. Worth finding out early, because it changes how scared you should be. (It won't erase things like GST or PAYG if those apply, so don't assume it's zero, but check.)

**Get a registered tax agent or accountant, ideally one who's worked with not-for-profits.** This is the step I'd push hardest on. An hour of a professional's time will tell you the real exposure and handle the disclosure properly. If you're in Australia, the ATO has a formal voluntary disclosure process, and an agent knows how to lodge it so you get the reduced-penalty treatment instead of stumbling into it.

**Take it to the committee, today or at the next meeting.** This is a governance matter, not your private burden to carry alone. Tell the president and the committee in writing, get it minuted, and get a decision recorded that the club is engaging an accountant to fix it. That protects you personally. As treasurer you have a duty to raise it; once you've raised it and the committee has resolved to act, the responsibility is the club's, not yours.

On personal liability, since I suspect that's the real fear under the panic: you didn't make these errors, and you're reporting them the moment you found them. Treasurers get into trouble for hiding problems or benefiting personally, not for cleaning up a predecessor's mistakes. Act openly, document as you go, and you're on the right side of this.

3.reply:
First: you're not in the trouble your gut thinks you are. Volunteer treasurers inherit messes constantly, and tax offices deal with corrected returns from small clubs all the time. The thing that turns an honest error into an actual problem is hiding it or dragging your feet, not the error itself. You found it, so you're already on the right side of that line.

Here's what to do, roughly in order.

**Stop and preserve.** Don't fix, delete, or "clean up" any of the old records yet. Whatever the previous treasurer left, leave it exactly as-is. You'll need the original mess to show what happened and when. Take backups if it's digital.

**Don't contact the tax office yet.** You can't disclose usefully until you know the size and shape of the problem. Contacting them mid-panic with "something's wrong, not sure how much" helps no one.

**Get the numbers straight.** Work out, year by year, what was actually earned versus what was reported, and roughly what the gap is. If it's beyond your comfort or the club can stretch to it, this is the moment to bring in a bookkeeper or accountant who works with not-for-profits. A few hundred dollars here saves you weeks of guessing.

**Check whether the "income" was even taxable.** This matters a lot and people miss it. Many small community sporting clubs in Australia are income-tax-exempt not-for-profits, and a chunk of club income (member subs, fees, fundraising among members) can be non-assessable under the mutuality principle. It's entirely possible the under-reported figure shrinks dramatically, or vanishes, once someone who knows NFP tax looks at it. Don't assume the worst until that's checked.

**Tell the committee.** You're a volunteer acting in good faith cleaning up someone else's error, and the club, not you personally, carries the liability. But the board needs to know now, in writing, with a plan attached. Handling it openly is also what protects you if anyone ever questions it later.

**Then make a voluntary disclosure.** Once you know the numbers, you (or your accountant) approach the ATO and correct it before they ever come asking. Voluntary disclosure gets you substantially reduced penalties, and often waived penalties for genuine mistakes, plus a payment arrangement if there's tax owing. This is the single biggest lever you have, and it only works while it's voluntary.

The realistic worst case for a small club is back-tax owed plus interest, paid off over time. Not fraud, not personal ruin. The reason to move steadily rather than frantically is that a calm, documented, self-reported fix is exactly the situation these processes are built to forgive.

One thing worth spending money on: an hour with an NFP accountant before you do anything else. They'll tell you in that hour whether you have a real liability or mostly a paperwork correction, and that answer changes everything about how hard the rest of this is.

You caught it. That's the hard part done.

4.reply:
First, breathe. You're going to be fine, and so is the club. What you're describing, inherited bookkeeping errors that a new treasurer found and flagged, is close to the least dangerous version of this problem. Tax offices reserve their teeth for people who hide things and keep hiding them. An honest club that comes forward gets treated completely differently.

Here's the straight version of what matters and what doesn't.

**You did not do this.** You discovered it. Keep those two things separate in your own head and in every record from here. The moment you found it, roughly what date, and how, is worth writing down today while it's fresh. That note protects you personally if anyone ever asks who knew what and when.

**Don't touch the books to "fix" it quietly.** No back-dating, no quiet adjustments to make the numbers reconcile. That's the one move that turns an error into something that looks deliberate. Leave the record as it is and correct it through the proper channel.

**Get the real numbers before you do anything else.** "Two years of under-reported income" could be a few hundred dollars or something bigger, and the right response scales with the amount. Pull the bank statements and reconstruct actual income for those two periods versus what was reported. You need the gap in dollars, not a feeling.

**One thing worth checking early:** small community sports clubs are often either tax-exempt or covered by the mutuality principle, where money members pay into their own club isn't taxable income at all. Depending on how the club is set up, some or all of that "under-reported income" may not have been taxable in the first place. Don't assume the worst-case tax bill until someone who knows the rules looks at it.

**Bring in a professional, and spend club money to do it.** A bookkeeper or accountant who handles not-for-profits will know the voluntary disclosure process cold. If you're in Australia (which I'm guessing), the ATO has a voluntary disclosure route, and coming forward before they find it typically cuts or removes penalties and shows good faith. This is a legitimate club expense. Don't wear it yourself.

**Tell the committee.** This is a governance matter, not your private crisis to solve alone. The board should know, approve engaging an accountant, and approve the disclosure. Put it in the minutes. You want the decision to correct this to be the club's decision, on the record, not something you did solo.

The order I'd actually work in: write your discovery note today, quantify the gap this week, loop in the committee chair, then engage an accountant to handle the disclosure. Nobody expects a volunteer treasurer to have caught someone else's two-year-old mistakes faster than you did.

What country is the club in, and do you have a rough sense of the dollar size of the gap? That changes how urgent the next step is.

5.reply:
First, the thing you need to hear: you didn't do this, and the fact that you found it and want to fix it is exactly what protects you. Treasurers who inherit a mess and report it are not the ones tax offices go after. The people who get punished are the ones who spot a problem and bury it. You're doing the opposite.

So slow your breathing down. This is a bookkeeping cleanup, not a scandal.

Here's what to actually do, roughly in order.

**Don't change anything in the old records.** Don't "fix" past entries, don't delete, don't tidy. Preserve the books exactly as the previous treasurer left them, plus your working notes on top. If anyone ever asks how this was found, you want a clean trail showing you discovered errors and disclosed them, not a set of quietly edited files.

**Get the scope before you get loud.** Right now you know something's wrong but probably not how wrong. Which years, how much income was missed, and was it genuinely omitted or just miscategorised. Sit down and reconstruct the actual numbers, or better, have someone do it with you. You can't disclose or fix what you haven't measured.

**Tell the committee, in writing, at the next meeting.** This is not yours to carry alone and it should never look like you sat on it. A short factual note to the board, minuted, saying what you found and that you're engaging an accountant to correct it. That does two things: it shares the responsibility where it belongs, and it timestamps that you acted the moment you knew.

**Engage a registered tax agent or accountant, and have the club pay for it.** Not a mate, not you Googling at midnight. Someone who handles not-for-profits or clubs specifically. They'll know the disclosure process and, more importantly, they'll do the talking to the tax office for you. The club's funds cover this. You should not be footing the bill personally for a predecessor's errors.

**The disclosure is the whole game, and voluntary is worth a lot.** Tax offices have formal voluntary-disclosure processes precisely for situations like this, and coming forward before they find it typically cuts penalties sharply, sometimes to near zero on the penalty side while you still pay the actual tax owed. Your accountant runs this. Prompt and voluntary beats late and cornered every time.

One more thing that might shrink this whole problem before you lose more sleep over it. A lot of small amateur sports clubs pay little or no income tax in the first place. If you're in Australia, many are income-tax-exempt as not-for-profit sporting bodies, and under the mutuality principle, money that members pay into their own club (subs, fees) often isn't assessable income at all. So the "under-reported income" may not have been taxable income to begin with. Don't take that as a promise, I don't know your club's structure or what the income was. But it's the first question to put to the accountant, because the answer could turn a two-year crisis into a filing correction.

What jurisdiction are you in, and roughly what kind of income got missed (member fees, canteen, sponsorship, grants)? Tell me that and I can point you at the specific disclosure process and whether the tax-exempt angle likely applies.

6.reply:
First, breathe. You didn't do this, and it's fixable. Tax offices deal with inherited bookkeeping messes constantly, and honest errors that get voluntarily corrected are treated very differently from anything hidden. Your panic is making this feel like a scandal. It's an admin problem with a known path through it.

Do not touch anything yet in a way you can't explain later. Don't quietly amend past filings on your own, don't delete or "clean up" records, don't back-date anything. The single most valuable thing you have right now is a clear paper trail showing you found the problem and acted on it properly. Write down today's date and what you discovered, in plain terms, and keep the original records exactly as they are.

A few things to work through, roughly in order.

Get the actual numbers before you assume the worst. "Two years of under-reporting" in your head might be a few hundred dollars of miscategorised membership fees, or it might be more. You can't make good decisions, or explain it to anyone, until you know the real figure. Pull the bank statements and reconcile against what was reported.

This isn't yours to carry alone. You're the treasurer of a club with a committee. Tell them, in writing, promptly. That protects you and it's genuinely their decision how to proceed, not just yours. If your club has a constitution or a president, this goes to them now, not after you've fixed it.

Get a professional in front of the numbers. An accountant or bookkeeper who works with small not-for-profits and sporting clubs specifically, not just any tax agent. Here's why that matters: many community sporting clubs are income-tax exempt as not-for-profits, and a lot of what looks like "unreported income" may not be taxable the way you're fearing, or the real exposure might be GST rather than income tax. I don't know your club's status and I'm not going to guess it for you. The point is the shape of the problem may be different from what's scaring you, and a specialist will tell you in one meeting.

Voluntary disclosure is your strongest card. If you're in Australia, the ATO has a formal voluntary disclosure process, and coming forward before they come to you cuts penalties substantially, sometimes to nothing on the honest-mistake end. Same principle in most countries. The accountant will handle the mechanics, but the reason to move now rather than sit on it is exactly this: the value of disclosing drops the moment it stops being voluntary.

On personal liability, because I suspect that's the real fear under the panic: you inherited errors made by someone else, you found them, and you're acting to correct them. That's the opposite of the behaviour that gets people in trouble. Document that timeline and you're in a defensible position. If the amounts turn out to be large, that's a question for an accountant and possibly a lawyer, not something to lose sleep over tonight.

What I'd actually do this week: reconcile the two years against the bank records so you know the number, put a short written note to your committee, and book one appointment with a not-for-profit accountant. That's the whole first move. Everything after that follows from what the numbers show.

You're doing the right thing by not ignoring it. That instinct is the one that keeps this small.

7.reply:
First: you're not in trouble for finding this. You're in trouble if you sit on it. Those are very different situations, and right now you're firmly in the first one.

Let me be straight about the actual risk. Honest bookkeeping errors that you inherited and are now correcting are close to the best-case version of a tax problem. This isn't fraud. You didn't do it, you found it, and you're moving to fix it. The tax office treats that completely differently from someone who hid income and got caught. In Australia the ATO has a formal voluntary disclosure process, and disclosing before they come to you cuts penalties sharply, sometimes to nothing on the penalty side. You'll still owe any actual shortfall plus interest, but the scary part, the penalties, is largely in your control and shrinks the sooner you speak up.

What to do this week:

**Don't touch the records to "fix" them yourself.** No backdating, no quietly amending old entries, no deleting anything. Preserve exactly what the previous treasurer left. If you alter the trail, a clean mistake starts to look like a cover-up. Just document what you've found.

**Tell the committee now, and get it minuted.** You're an officer of the club. You have both a duty to raise this and a right not to carry it alone. Put it in writing to the president/chair today, factually, no drama. That minute is also your protection: it shows the moment you knew, you acted.

**Get the numbers before you get scared.** You don't actually know the size of this yet. "Two years of errors" could be a few hundred dollars or something material, and the difference changes everything. Engage a bookkeeper or accountant to reconstruct the two years and tell you the real figure. Most will understand this is a volunteer NFP situation.

**Then make the voluntary disclosure** through the accountant, with the numbers in hand.

Two things that might matter more than you think, which is why I want to ask before assuming the worst:

1. Is your club a not-for-profit sporting organisation? Many community sports clubs are self-assessed income-tax exempt. If yours is, "under-reporting income" may have little or no income-tax consequence at all, and the real issue is something else (often GST, if turnover crossed the NFP threshold).
2. What tax are we actually talking about? Income tax, GST, PAYG withholding on any paid staff? They carry different weights.

So, two questions: roughly how big is the discrepancy (order of magnitude, not exact), and is the club registered as an NFP / does it have staff on payroll? Your answers change what "fixing this" looks like.

One honest caveat: I'm not your accountant or lawyer, and I'd want a professional to look at the actual books before you file anything. But nothing above requires you to panic tonight. The situation is stable, the errors are old, and you have time to do this properly. The clock that matters, disclosing before the ATO finds it on their own, is one you're currently winning.

8.reply:
First thing: you didn't do this. You found it. Those are two different situations and the tax office treats them completely differently. A volunteer who inherits a mess, spots it, and moves to fix it properly is close to the lowest-risk position there is. Panic is making this feel like your crime. It isn't.

Now, the practical order of operations.

**Don't touch the old records or quietly "correct" anything.** No back-dating, no adjusting prior entries to make them line up. Leave the history as it is and document what you found in a separate note with today's date. The moment records look altered, an honest error starts to look like concealment. Keep the trail clean.

**Before you assume there's a tax debt at all, check whether the "income" was even taxable.** This is the part that might dissolve most of your fear. In Australia, a lot of community sports clubs pay little or no income tax because of two things: not-for-profit status, and the mutuality principle. Under mutuality, money the club collects from its own members (subs, member match fees, member social events) generally isn't assessable income at all. Only genuinely external money tends to count: bar or canteen sales to the public, sponsorships, grants of certain kinds, interest. So "we under-reported income for two years" could mean the club under-reported a number that was never taxable in the first place. You need to know the mix before you know the size of any problem. It may be far smaller than the raw figure, or zero.

**Get the real numbers reconstructed by someone who does this for clubs.** You want a bookkeeper or accountant with not-for-profit and sporting-club experience, not a general one. Two years of errors need to be untangled into actual member vs non-member income, GST if the club is registered, and whatever the club's tax position genuinely is. You cannot disclose a number you don't yet trust.

**Take it to the committee now, and get it minuted.** This is the step people skip because they're embarrassed, and it's the one that protects you most. You are the treasurer, not the owner of the club's liabilities. Put it in front of the president and committee in writing: here's what I found, here's what I propose (engage an accountant, then make a voluntary disclosure), can I have approval to spend on professional advice. Once that's a recorded committee decision, you're a volunteer executing a club resolution, not a lone person making unilateral calls about a tax matter.

**Then make a voluntary disclosure to the ATO, once you have real figures.** This is the lever that matters. Disclosing before the ATO comes asking cuts penalties dramatically, and for honest errors that are voluntarily corrected the outcome is usually amended returns plus any shortfall, often with penalties reduced or remitted. The accountant handles the mechanics and the wording. Do not cold-call the ATO yourself in a panic with half the picture.

On your personal exposure: for a volunteer acting in good faith who surfaces the problem and follows advice, it's very low. Personal liability for club tax debts generally needs something like dishonesty or serious recklessness, which is the opposite of what you're doing. Acting fast and on the record is exactly what keeps it that way.

I'm not your accountant or lawyer, and the specifics turn on the club's structure, whether it's incorporated, and whether it's GST-registered. So treat this as the map, not the legal advice. But the shape is clear: stop, preserve the records, find out whether it's even taxable, get the committee behind you in writing, get a club-savvy accountant, disclose voluntarily.

Do the committee email today, even a short one. That single step moves this off your shoulders and onto the club's, which is where it actually belongs. Then sleep. This is a fixable bookkeeping problem, not a scandal.

9.reply:
First, you're not in trouble. You inherited someone else's mistake and you found it. That's the opposite of the situation the tax office actually goes after.

Let me be straight about the shape of this, because the panic is mostly fear of the unknown.

Honest bookkeeping errors that get voluntarily disclosed are a routine, boring event for a tax office. What gets people penalised hard is concealment, or getting caught in an audit before they come clean. You're on the good side of that line as long as you act like it. So the worst thing you can do right now is nothing, and the second worst is quietly "fixing" the numbers without a paper trail.

A few things before you spiral further:

**You may not even owe what you think.** Small community sports clubs in Australia are very often income-tax exempt. A not-for-profit sporting body can self-assess as exempt, and on top of that the mutuality principle means money from members (subs, membership fees) usually isn't assessable income at all. So "under-reported income" could be income the club was never going to be taxed on. It might turn out the real exposure is GST, or nothing. Don't assume the number in your head is a tax bill. It might be zero.

**You personally are almost certainly not on the hook** for the previous treasurer's errors, as long as you handle it properly once you know. Personal liability for committee members comes from things like ignoring a known problem or acting dishonestly. Finding it and reporting it is the defence, not the risk.

Here's what I'd actually do, roughly in order:

1. **Don't touch or delete anything.** Preserve the old records exactly as they are, errors included. You want to be able to show what happened, not have it look like you scrubbed it.

2. **Get the facts before you act.** Which two financial years. What type of income. Roughly how much. Whether the club has ever registered as tax-exempt or for GST. You need the size and shape of this before anyone can tell you if it's a $0 problem or a real one.

3. **Tell your committee, in writing, now.** A short factual note to the president/chair: what you found, that you're getting advice, that you'll report it properly. This protects you and it's the right thing. You don't carry this alone, and you shouldn't be the only person who knows.

4. **Get a registered tax agent or accountant** who works with not-for-profits. This is the step that actually resolves it, and it's worth the club paying for. They'll tell you whether there's any liability at all, and if there is, they handle the voluntary disclosure. A club your size, this is likely a couple of hours of their time.

5. **Voluntary disclosure is your friend.** If it turns out something is genuinely owed, coming forward before any audit typically cuts penalties dramatically, sometimes to little more than interest. The mechanism is designed for exactly your situation: honest error, self-reported.

The realistic outcome here is not ruin. It's a corrected set of returns, possibly a small interest charge, possibly nothing, and a club that's now on cleaner footing than it was under the last treasurer.

One question that changes the advice a lot: does the club know whether it's ever been treated as income-tax exempt, and is it registered for GST? If you can tell me that plus rough figures, I can help you figure out whether you're looking at a real liability or a scare, and I can draft the note to your committee if that would take one thing off your plate.

10.reply:
First, breathe. What you're describing is recoverable, and you're already doing the one thing that matters most: you found it and you're not sitting on it.

Let me be straight with you about where you actually stand, because the panic is running ahead of the facts.

**You are not personally on the hook for your predecessor's honest mistakes.** You inherited a mess you didn't make, you spotted it, and you're moving to fix it. That's the opposite of the situation the ATO comes down hard on. The people who get hurt are the ones who knew and hid it, or who kept under-reporting after they understood. Neither is you.

**Voluntary disclosure is your biggest lever, and it's still available.** The ATO treats "we found an error and came to you" completely differently from "we caught you." Coming forward before they contact you can reduce or wipe the shortfall penalties, and it reads as good faith rather than evasion. That advantage only exists while you're the one making the first move, so the clock favours acting soon, not rushing today.

**Check whether the club even owes the tax you think it does.** A lot of small community sporting clubs are income-tax exempt as not-for-profits. If yours qualifies, then "under-reported income" may carry little or no actual tax liability, and this whole thing shrinks to a paperwork and record-keeping fix. Don't assume the worst-case number in your head is the real number. It usually isn't.

Here's what I'd do, roughly in order:

- **Freeze the old records. Don't correct, tidy, or delete anything** in the previous treasurer's books. You want the original mess preserved exactly as you found it. Your fix goes on top, documented, not over the top.
- **Tell the committee now, in writing, and get it minuted.** This is a club governance matter, not a weight you carry alone. The minute that says "treasurer identified and reported historical errors on [date]" also protects you personally. Do this within the week.
- **Get the real scope from someone who knows NFPs.** A bookkeeper or accountant who works with community clubs will cost a few hundred dollars and is worth every cent. They'll tell you the actual exposure, whether the club is exempt, what needs lodging, and how to word the disclosure. Do not self-report a panic estimate to the ATO, wrong numbers create new problems.
- **Then make the voluntary disclosure**, ideally with the accountant guiding it. There's a proper channel for exactly this, amending prior lodgements or disclosing shortfalls.

One more thing. Keep a short file of your own: when you discovered it, what you found, who you told, what you did next. Not because anyone's coming after you, but because the paper trail is what turns "the club had a problem" into "the treasurer handled it properly."

I'm not an accountant and this isn't formal advice, so let a NFP-experienced one confirm the specifics for your club's structure. But you're closer to the finish line than the panic is telling you. Discovered, disclosed, fixed. That's the whole path, and you're already on step one.

11.reply:
First thing, and I mean this literally: breathe. What you're describing is one of the most ordinary situations a tax office deals with. A volunteer inherits a mess, finds honest errors, and moves to fix them. That is not fraud, it's not what gets people in serious trouble, and nobody is coming after a treasurer who discovered a problem and reported it. The danger in these situations is almost never the original error. It's sitting on it once you know.

So here's the order I'd work in.

**Check whether the club owes income tax at all.** Before you assume two years of back-tax, find out if the club is even a taxable entity. A lot of amateur community sporting clubs are income-tax exempt as not-for-profits, under the "encouragement of a game or sport" category. If yours qualifies, "under-reported income" might not convert into a tax bill the way you're picturing. One catch worth knowing: since the 2023-24 year, non-charitable NFPs with an ABN have to lodge an annual self-review return to hold onto that exemption, and heaps of small clubs missed the memo. So the question underneath the bookkeeping is really the club's tax status. Pin that down first, because it decides whether the rest is a big problem or a paperwork clean-up. (I'm assuming the ATO here given where you are. If it's a different country the principles hold, the names change.)

If GST is the issue rather than income tax, that's a separate track. NFPs generally only have to be registered for GST once turnover passes $150k, so check whether the club was even over that line.

**Tell your committee now, in writing.** This is not yours to carry alone, and you don't want to be the treasurer who quietly handled a tax matter without the board knowing. Email the president or chair today, plain and factual: what you found, roughly which period, that it predates you, and that you're getting proper advice. Get it into the next set of minutes. That protects the club and it protects you.

**Get the numbers straight before you talk to anyone official.** Pull the bank statements, the old returns, whatever records exist, and work out the actual gap year by year. You can't disclose or fix what you can't quantify. This is also where a real answer replaces the worst-case one in your head, which right now is probably scarier than the reality.

**Bring in an accountant who knows not-for-profits.** Not a generalist. NFP tax is its own thing and a good one will tell you in an hour whether you're looking at a genuine liability or a status fix. The fee is a legitimate club expense. Have them handle any contact with the tax office.

**Voluntary disclosure is your friend, not a confession.** If there is a real liability, coming forward before the ATO finds it is the single biggest lever on the outcome. Voluntary disclosures on honest errors typically see penalties heavily reduced, often to nil for something small and unintentional. The people who get hammered are the ones who got caught hiding it. You are the opposite of that.

On the fear I suspect is loudest: you are almost certainly not personally on the hook. A volunteer acting honestly, who inherited the error and disclosed it, is not who liability lands on. Your protection is exactly the thing you're doing right now, surfacing it instead of burying it.

I'm not your accountant and the specifics turn on your club's structure and whether it's incorporated, so treat this as the shape of it rather than the final word. But the path is clear enough: confirm the tax status, tell the committee, quantify the gap, get NFP-savvy advice, disclose if needed. Do those and the worst version of this doesn't happen.

You caught something the last person didn't. That's you doing the job well, not badly.

12.reply:
First: you're not in trouble. You inherited someone else's mistake and you're catching it. That's the opposite of the situation tax authorities actually punish.

The thing to hold onto is that under-reporting caught and reported by you voluntarily is treated very differently from under-reporting they find in an audit. Most tax offices have a voluntary disclosure process, and using it before they come knocking usually means reduced or waived penalties, sometimes just the tax owed plus interest. The people who get hammered are the ones who knew and sat on it. You're doing neither.

So don't rush to "confess" today in a panic email. Do this in order.

**Stop touching the books solo.** You're a volunteer, not a forensic accountant, and anything you reconstruct wrong now makes the cleanup harder. Preserve what exists exactly as it is: bank statements, the old treasurer's ledgers or spreadsheets, receipts, prior returns. Don't correct, don't delete, don't "tidy."

**Get the committee in the loop early.** This is a club liability, not a personal one, and you should not be the only person who knows. Tell the chair or president now, in writing, keep it factual: previous treasurer's records appear to have under-reported income across roughly two years, you're getting it assessed. That protects you and makes it a club decision, which it is. Most clubs also have an obligation to their members here, and possibly a rule about reporting to the committee.

**Bring in an accountant, ideally one who works with community clubs or not-for-profits.** This is the real fix. They'll quantify the actual shortfall (your panic number is probably not the real number), tell you exactly which years and which returns need amending, and file the voluntary disclosure properly. A couple hundred in fees here saves you from guessing wrong on a tax filing, which is the one place you really don't want to guess.

**Write down the timeline while it's fresh.** When you took over, when you found it, what tipped you off, what you did next. If anyone ever asks, this is your record that you acted the moment you knew.

One straight thing on the previous treasurer: assume incompetence, not fraud, until an accountant says otherwise. Two years of the same error usually means someone didn't understand what counted as income, not that they were skimming. Either way that's a question for after you've fixed the filings, not now.

What country is the club in? The disclosure process and the club's reporting duties differ by jurisdiction, and I can point you at the right specifics once I know. And roughly how big are we talking, a few hundred a year or a lot more? That changes how urgently you need the accountant versus whether you can breathe for a week and book one properly.

13.reply:
First, the thing you need to hear: you're almost certainly not in the trouble your gut is telling you you're in. You inherited this. You found it. Volunteers who report a predecessor's honest mistakes get treated completely differently from people who hide their own, and that difference shapes how the whole thing ends.

Now, in order.

**Don't fix anything yet.** Don't edit old spreadsheets, re-date entries, or "tidy up" the prior years. Leave every record exactly as it is, wrong numbers included. You want a clean trail showing what happened and when you caught it. Altering records is the one move that turns an error into something that looks deliberate.

**Work out the actual size of it.** Before you talk to anyone official, get real figures: how much income was under-reported, in which years, and roughly what tax that comes to. You can't disclose or decide anything sensibly while it's still a vague dread. Might be a few hundred dollars. Might be more. You need to know which.

**Tell the committee this week, in writing.** You're a volunteer treasurer, not the club itself. The liability sits with the club as an organisation, and how to handle it is a decision for the whole committee, not something you carry alone. Send the chair a short, factual note and get it into the minutes. That record also protects you personally if anyone ever asks whether you sat on it.

**Get an accountant who knows community clubs.** Not just any accountant, one who works with not-for-profits and sporting bodies. Worth paying for even on a tight club budget, and it might shrink the whole problem. Small member-based sports clubs often owe far less than you'd assume. If you're in Australia, a lot of club income (member subs and similar) isn't assessable at all under the mutuality principle, and many clubs sit as income-tax-exempt not-for-profits. Two years of "under-reported income" can resolve to a tiny liability, or none, once someone qualified actually runs the numbers. Don't assume the scariest figure is the real one.

**Then disclose voluntarily.** Every major tax authority (the ATO, HMRC, the IRS, whichever is yours) runs a voluntary disclosure process, and they cut penalties sharply for people who come forward before an audit finds them. Coming to them early is worth real money. Getting found first is the expensive road.

One thing that changes the specifics: which country's tax office? That decides the exact disclosure process and whether the exemption angle applies.

You caught this, and you're moving on it. That's the good version of this story, not the bad one.

14.reply:
Breathe. Nobody's coming to arrest you. Under-reported club income discovered by an incoming volunteer treasurer is a routine correction, not a scandal, and you didn't cause it.

Here's the actual situation. The errors were made by someone else, over two years, and you found them. That's the opposite of the profile the tax office cares about. What they come down hard on is deliberate concealment. Honest mistakes that get voluntarily disclosed sit at the far gentle end of the spectrum, especially for a small not-for-profit sports club where the amounts are almost certainly modest.

What to do, in order:

**Stop and preserve, don't fix yet.** Don't start amending records or "cleaning up" the books tonight. Leave the errors exactly as they are and document what you found: which years, roughly how much income was missed, and how you spotted it. If you patch things silently now, you lose the paper trail that proves this was an honest error found and disclosed.

**Tell the committee before you tell anyone else.** You're a volunteer treasurer, not the club. The decision to disclose and how to fund any shortfall belongs to the committee (or the board/president). Put it in front of them in writing, plainly. Get it minuted. This also protects you personally, so it's a club decision, not a solo move.

**Get a bookkeeper or accountant who knows NFPs, ideally before contacting the tax office.** You need someone to quantify the real number and confirm what the club's obligations even are. Many small sports clubs are income-tax exempt or mutual, and a lot of "income" (member subscriptions, some fundraising) isn't taxable the way you might assume. It's genuinely possible the shortfall is far smaller than you fear, or close to nil. Spending a few hundred dollars on an hour of professional time here is the single highest-value thing you can do.

**Then make a voluntary disclosure.** If money is genuinely owed, the club (through the accountant) approaches the ATO first, before they ever come asking. In Australia that's a voluntary disclosure, and it substantially reduces or removes penalties and shows good faith. Coming forward is treated very differently from being caught.

Two things to get straight in your head:

You are not personally liable for tax the club owes, and you're not liable for your predecessor's mistakes. Volunteer committee members acting in good faith have strong protections. The thing that could expose you personally is knowing about an error and hiding it, which is exactly what you're not doing.

And the amount is probably smaller than your panic is telling you. Two years of a small club's under-reported income, once you strip out non-taxable member money, is often a few hundred to a few thousand dollars, if anything is owed at all.

One question that changes the advice: do you know roughly what the club's tax status is, whether it's registered as income-tax exempt, or a registered charity, or just a plain incorporated association? Tell me that and I can point you at what actually applies.

You did the right thing by catching it. Now it's a process, not an emergency.

15.reply:
First, breathe. This is recoverable, and you're already doing the one thing that matters: you found it and you're not hiding it. Honest bookkeeping errors that you disclose voluntarily are a completely different animal from concealment the tax office uncovers itself. The panic you're feeling is about the second scenario. You're in the first.

You said "tax office," and your situation reads Australian, so I'll answer for the ATO. If you're somewhere else, tell me and I'll adjust, because the specifics change.

**Start with the question that might dissolve most of this: is the club even a taxable entity?** A lot of small community sporting clubs aren't. Non-profit clubs established to encourage a game or sport can self-assess as income tax exempt under the ATO's rules, meaning they lodge nothing and owe nothing on that income. If your club falls in that category, then "under-reporting income" for two years may carry no tax liability at all, and this shrinks from a crisis to a paperwork correction. Before you assume the worst, find out what the club's actual tax status is. Check whether it's registered as a charity with the ACNC, whether it has an ABN, whether it's ever lodged returns, and whether prior treasurers treated it as exempt. This is the first thing an accountant will look at too.

Now the practical moves, in order:

**Don't touch or "tidy up" the old records.** Preserve everything exactly as the previous treasurer left it. Bank statements, spreadsheets, receipts, the lot. If you start quietly amending historical books, you turn an inherited error into something that looks like you altering records. Correct going forward through the proper channel, not by rewriting the past.

**Pin down the scope before you do anything else.** Which income was missed, roughly how much per year, and which financial years. You don't need it to the dollar yet, just the shape of it. That determines whether this is a rounding issue or something that needs a formal amendment.

**Get a registered tax agent, ideally one who works with not-for-profits.** This is worth the few hundred dollars for a small club. They'll confirm the club's tax status, tell you whether anything is actually owed, and if a disclosure is needed they'll handle the wording. Community clubs deal with exactly this more often than you'd think.

**If tax is owed, the mechanism is a voluntary disclosure.** Coming to the ATO before they come to you cuts penalties substantially, and for a genuine, disclosed error on a small non-profit the outcome is usually a corrected return plus interest, not punishment. The ATO distinguishes hard between mistakes and evasion.

**Tell your committee now, in writing.** Don't carry this alone, and don't let it look later like you knew and sat on it. A short, factual note to the president or committee: what you found, when you found it, and that you're getting professional advice. That record protects you.

On your personal exposure, since I suspect that's part of the fear: you did not create these errors, and a volunteer treasurer isn't personally liable for tax an incorporated club owes. Your duty is to act reasonably once you know, which is precisely what you're doing. The way a volunteer gets into trouble is by concealing or ignoring, not by inheriting someone else's mistake and fixing it.

Tell me the jurisdiction and roughly how much income we're talking about per year, and whether the club is incorporated and has an ABN. I can get more specific from there.

16.reply:
First: you're not in trouble for finding this. The person who discovers and reports an error is in a completely different position from the person who made it or hid it. Tax offices deal with exactly this situation all the time, especially with volunteer-run clubs. It's a correction process, not a criminal one, as long as you handle it openly.

So don't panic-fix anything. Right now, before you do anything else:

Don't alter, delete, or "tidy up" any of the old records. Leave them exactly as they are. Whatever mess is in there is your evidence that this was an honest bookkeeping error, not concealment. If you start editing historical files it starts to look like a cover-up, which is the one thing that actually creates personal risk.

Now the real work, roughly in order:

**Check whether tax was even owed.** This is the step most panicking treasurers skip. Small community sports clubs are very often income-tax exempt or sit under special rules for not-for-profits and amateur sporting bodies. If your club qualifies, "under-reported income" may mean the bookkeeping was wrong but the actual tax payable was nil or close to it. Find out the club's tax status before you assume there's a debt. That single fact can shrink this from a crisis to a paperwork job.

**Quantify it, roughly.** You don't need a perfect number yet. You need a defensible estimate of how much income went unrecorded across the two years, so you know the scale. Bank statements are your friend here because they don't lie the way a handwritten ledger might.

**Get a professional involved.** An accountant or registered tax agent who works with not-for-profits. This is worth paying for, and the club should cover it, not you. They'll tell you the real exposure, handle the amendments, and manage the tax office contact so you're not doing it blind. If money is tight, many will do a short paid consult just to scope it.

**Make a voluntary disclosure.** This is the part that protects everyone. In most tax systems, coming forward before the tax office finds the problem itself dramatically reduces penalties, sometimes to near zero, and shifts the whole thing into "cooperative correction" territory. The advantage evaporates the moment they contact you first. Your accountant will frame this properly, but the direction is clear: you go to them, they don't come to you.

Two things about protecting yourself:

Tell the committee or board now, in writing, and put it on the record at the next meeting. This is the club's liability, not your personal one, and your job as treasurer is to surface it, not to quietly absorb it. A short factual email today ("I've found bookkeeping errors from 2024 and 2025 that appear to have under-reported income; I'm getting professional advice and recommend we make a voluntary disclosure") does two jobs at once. It gets you help and it timestamps that you acted the moment you knew.

Don't go after the previous treasurer. It's tempting, but for now they're almost certainly guilty of nothing worse than being an overwhelmed volunteer with no accounting training. Unless you find evidence of actual theft (money missing, not just miscounted), treat this as an error to correct, not a person to blame. If real money is missing, that's a separate and more serious conversation, and that's when you'd want proper advice before doing anything.

The honest summary: this is stressful but it is genuinely routine, it is fixable, and the facts are on your side because you found it and you're moving to fix it fast. Get an accountant, tell the committee this week, and don't touch the old records.

If you tell me roughly which country the club is in and its rough annual income, I can point you at the specific exemption rules and disclosure process that apply, so you walk into that accountant meeting already knowing the shape of it.

17.reply:
First, breathe. You're going to be fine, and so is the club. Under-reporting caused by a predecessor's sloppy books is one of the most ordinary things a tax office sees. It is not fraud when you find an honest error and move to correct it. The thing that turns a mistake into a real problem is hiding it, and you're doing the opposite.

Here's what actually matters right now.

**Don't touch the old records.** No cleaning up, no back-dating, no "fixing" entries to make them tidy. Preserve everything exactly as the previous treasurer left it: the bank statements, the spreadsheets, the receipts, whatever exists. If you alter records while sorting this out, an honest correction can start to look like a cover-up. Take copies before you work on anything.

**Don't report numbers you haven't verified.** The instinct when panicking is to rush a corrected figure to the tax office today. Resist that. A wrong correction creates a second mess. You need to know the real shortfall first.

**Get a registered tax agent or an accountant who works with community clubs.** This is the one thing I'd push you on. Not to hand off the panic, but because two years of errors need someone who can reconstruct the position and calculate what's actually owed. Many will do a reduced rate or pro bono for a volunteer-run sports club. The cost of getting this wrong is far higher than their fee.

**Voluntary disclosure is your friend.** In most systems, including Australia's ATO if that's who you're dealing with, coming forward yourself before they contact you cuts the penalties substantially, sometimes to near nothing on the penalty side. You still pay the tax that was genuinely owed, plus interest, but the punitive part shrinks a lot when the disclosure is voluntary. Your accountant will handle the mechanics, and the timing (before any audit notice) is what earns the reduction.

On the fear I suspect is underneath this: as a volunteer treasurer acting in good faith, you are generally not personally liable for the club's tax debt. The club is the entity that owes it. Personal exposure only tends to appear where there's dishonesty or reckless disregard, and finding an error and reporting it is the exact opposite of that. Check your club's constitution and whether it has office-holder indemnity or insurance, but don't let a nightmare about your own bank account drive your decisions.

Two more things, then I'll stop.

Tell the club committee or president in writing, soon. You didn't cause this, and you want it on record that you discovered it and acted. A short factual note: what you found, when, and that you're engaging an accountant to correct it. That protects you and it's the honest move.

Write down the timeline for yourself while it's fresh. When you took over, when you noticed, what you saw. That record is worth a lot later.

The order that keeps you safe: preserve records, get an accountant, verify the real number, then make a voluntary disclosure with their help. You've already done the hardest part, which is not looking away. What's your rough sense of the club's size and which country's tax office this is? I can point you at the specific voluntary disclosure process from there.

18.reply:
First, breathe. This is recoverable, and the situation you're describing is one the tax office deals with constantly. Honest bookkeeping errors by a predecessor are not fraud, and you didn't make them. What you do next is what matters.

Here's the straight version.

**You are not personally on the hook for this.** A volunteer treasurer acting in good faith, who finds a problem and reports it, is in about the safest position you can be in. The danger with tax problems is never the original mistake. It's concealment after you know. So the moment you decided to deal with it honestly, you moved yourself onto the right side of the line. Document that you discovered it and when.

**Slow down before you quantify anything.** One thing worth checking early: a lot of small community sports clubs in Australia don't actually pay income tax. Not-for-profit clubs "established for the encouragement of a game or sport" can self-assess as income-tax exempt, in which case they don't lodge an income tax return at all. So before you assume the worst, work out what the "income" was actually being reported for. The three real possibilities:

- **GST / BAS** — if the club is registered for GST (required once turnover hits $150k for a not-for-profit). This is the most likely culprit for a small club, and under-reported income here means under-paid GST.
- **Income tax** — only if the club isn't exempt.
- **PAYG** — only if the club pays wages.

Knowing which one changes the size of the problem enormously. It might be smaller than the panic suggests.

**The steps, in order:**

1. Pull the records. Bank statements, prior BAS lodgements or returns, the previous treasurer's books. Get the two years in front of you.
2. Get a bookkeeper or accountant who works with not-for-profits to reconstruct the actual numbers. Do not try to nail the exact figure yourself while stressed. You need a defensible number, not a guess.
3. Tell your committee. This is a governance matter, not a solo one, and the decision to correct it should be minuted. It also protects you.
4. Make a voluntary disclosure to the ATO. This is the important one. When you come to them before they come to you, penalties are reduced heavily and sometimes waived entirely for genuine mistakes. The difference between voluntary disclosure and getting caught is the whole ballgame for penalties and interest.
5. Check the club's obligations to your state regulator too (incorporated associations report to the state fair trading / consumer affairs body, separate from the ATO). Your constitution may require the committee be formally notified.

**One caution:** don't contact the ATO with numbers until the accountant has reconstructed them. Approaching them is right; approaching them with wrong figures creates a second mess. Get the number solid first, then disclose promptly.

Two things I can't tell you without more detail: whether your club is income-tax exempt, and whether the issue is GST or income tax. If you tell me which the club actually lodges (a BAS? an income tax return? nothing?) and roughly the annual turnover, I can give you a sharper read on how serious this actually is and what the disclosure looks like.

You caught it. You're acting on it. That's the treasurer doing the job right, not failing at it.

19.reply:
First, breathe. You didn't make these errors, and you found them, which is the single most important fact in the whole situation. Tax offices treat "the new treasurer found a mistake and came forward" completely differently from "we caught you hiding income." You're on the right side of that line as long as you act sensibly now.

A few things not to do. Don't start editing or "cleaning up" the old records to make them look tidy, that turns an honest error into something that looks like a cover-up. Don't quietly fix future returns and hope the past goes away. And don't fire off a disclosure to the tax office tonight while you're panicking, because you'll get the numbers wrong.

Here's the order I'd work in.

Tell the committee first. You are a volunteer acting for the club, not personally on the hook for a predecessor's bookkeeping. This is a club governance matter and the committee needs to know today or at the next meeting, in writing (an email or minuted note). That protects you and makes it a shared decision, which is exactly what it should be.

Stop and preserve. Freeze the records as they are. Bank statements, the old ledgers or spreadsheets, receipts, whatever the previous treasurer left. Don't alter anything. If it's a shared accounting file, take a copy and work in the copy.

Get the real numbers. This is where you want a professional. A bookkeeper or accountant who works with small clubs and not-for-profits can reconstruct the two years, tell you the actual under-reported amount, and tell you whether it even crosses a threshold that matters (small community clubs often have concessions or exemptions, and the shortfall may be smaller than your panic is telling you). Paying for a few hours of this is worth it, and the club should fund it, not you.

Then make a voluntary disclosure. Most tax offices, the ATO included if you're in Australia, have a formal voluntary-disclosure route. Coming forward before they contact you almost always means reduced or waived penalties, and interest is usually the main remaining cost. Your accountant will lodge the amendments and the disclosure together so the story is clean: here's what was wrong, here's the corrected figure, here's the payment.

On personal liability, since I suspect that's the real fear: a volunteer treasurer acting in good faith to correct an inherited error is in a very different position from someone who benefited or concealed. If the club has any incorporated structure or insurance, check whether there's association liability cover. But the behaviour you're describing, finding it and fixing it, is the protective behaviour.

Two honest caveats. I don't know your country or the club's exact tax status, so treat the above as the shape of the plan, not legal advice, and let the accountant fill in the specifics. And get the committee's acknowledgment in writing before you spend club money or lodge anything, so you're never the person who "decided this alone."

You've got a bad inheritance, not a crisis of your own making. Want me to help you draft the note to the committee, or a plain-English summary you can hand an accountant so the first meeting is fast?

20.reply:
First, breathe. This is fixable, and it is not the disaster it feels like right now.

The thing to hold onto: an honest bookkeeping error by your predecessor, found and voluntarily reported by you, sits in a completely different category from tax evasion. Tax offices see this constantly with small clubs run by volunteers. What they punish hard is concealment. What you're about to do is the opposite of concealment.

Here's the order I'd work in.

**Don't act alone, and don't act yet.** You're a volunteer, not the club. Before anything goes to the tax office, the committee or board needs to know and needs to authorize the response. This protects you personally and it's almost certainly required under your club's rules. Call an emergency committee meeting or at minimum brief the chair/president today.

**Preserve everything, change nothing.** Do not "fix" the old records, backdate corrections, or clean anything up. Leave the erroneous books as they are and make corrections as clearly dated new entries. Altering historical records is the one move that can turn a mistake into something that looks deliberate.

**Get the numbers before you get scared.** Right now you have a shape of a problem, not a figure. You need to know: which two years, how much income was missed each year, and what the actual tax shortfall is (many small sports clubs have exemptions or concessions that mean the shortfall is smaller than the missed income, sometimes zero). Don't try to nail this alone. A bookkeeper or accountant who works with community/not-for-profit clubs can quantify it fast, and you want a professional's numbers when you disclose.

**Then make a voluntary disclosure.** Most tax authorities have a formal voluntary disclosure process, and coming forward before they contact you typically cuts penalties sharply and can remove them entirely for genuine errors. Interest on the underpaid amount usually still applies, but that's a cost, not a catastrophe. The accountant can lodge this with you or on the club's behalf.

A few straight answers to what you're probably actually worried about:

Are you personally on the hook? Almost certainly not, if you act in good faith. You inherited this, you didn't cause it, and you reported it the moment you understood it. That's the behaviour that protects a volunteer. Document what you found and when, so the timeline is clear.

Is the club in serious trouble? Unlikely to be existential. Underpaid tax plus interest, possibly a reduced penalty, on a small club's income is usually a manageable number, not a shutdown event.

Should you get a lawyer? Not first. Start with an accountant. Escalate to legal advice only if the amounts are large or the tax office signals it thinks this was deliberate, which, given the facts as you describe them, it shouldn't.

One thing I can't give you cleanly: the exact disclosure mechanism and penalty rules depend on your country. Tell me where the club is and I'll get more specific about the actual process and who to call.

The move today is small and calm: message the chair, and line up a not-for-profit accountant for this week. You've already done the hardest part, which is not looking away.

21.reply:
First: you're not in the trouble you think you're in. You found the problem, you didn't cause it, and you're reporting it. That's the opposite of what people who get punished do.

Let me be straight about the actual risk. Tax offices distinguish hard between two things: someone who hid income and got caught, and someone who found an old error and came forward. The first gets penalties and sometimes worse. The second usually gets the tax owed plus interest, with penalties reduced or waived because the disclosure was voluntary. You are firmly in the second category, and the fact that it was your predecessor's error, not yours, helps you further.

So don't make the panic decision, which is either to quietly "fix it going forward" and say nothing, or to fire off a confession tonight with half the facts. Both are wrong. Here's the order that protects you and the club.

**Preserve everything, change nothing.** Do not edit, delete, or "clean up" the old records. Whatever the previous treasurer left, bank statements, spreadsheets, receipts, the accounting file, leave it exactly as is. If you correct errors in the live books, do it as dated new entries, not by overwriting history. You may need to show the original state later, and altered records look far worse than the original mistake.

**Quantify before you disclose.** You can't disclose responsibly until you know roughly how much income was under-reported and across which periods. Two years, which specific returns, what the correct figures should have been. Get this reasonably solid first. A vague "we think we got it wrong" to the tax office is weaker than "here are the two years, here's the corrected income, here's the tax we believe is owed."

**This is the committee's problem, not yours alone.** You're a volunteer, not the club. Tell the president or chair now, and get it minuted that you raised it and when. The decision to disclose, and to pay for professional help, belongs to the committee. Putting it on record also protects you personally: you found it and escalated it, in writing, promptly.

**Get an accountant, ideally one who works with clubs or not-for-profits.** This is the one thing worth spending money on. They'll size the liability correctly, know the specific voluntary-disclosure process for your tax office, and often deal with the tax office on the club's behalf, which lands better than a panicked treasurer doing it solo. Many not-for-profits also have concessions or thresholds that might change the picture entirely.

One thing I need from you before giving you the exact mechanism: which country's tax office is this? The voluntary disclosure process, the penalty relief, and the deadlines are different in Australia (ATO), the UK (HMRC), the US (IRS), and elsewhere. If you tell me, I'll walk you through that specific path.

The mistake is real but it's ordinary and it's fixable. Clubs do this and survive it all the time. What determines the outcome now is how you handle the next two weeks, and so far you're doing the right thing by taking it seriously instead of burying it.

22.reply:
Breathe. This is a fixable problem, and the fact that you found it and want to deal with it straight is the thing that matters most to a tax office. Honest bookkeeping errors and fraud are treated very differently. You're in the first camp.

Here's the order I'd work in.

**Don't touch the old records to "fix" them.** Preserve everything exactly as it is: the files, the bank statements, the spreadsheets, whatever the previous treasurer left. If anyone later asks, you want a clean story of "found it, quantified it, disclosed it," not edits that could look like someone covering tracks. Make copies, work from the copies.

**Write down what you know, today, while it's fresh.** When you discovered it, what tipped you off, which years are affected, roughly how. A dated file for yourself. This protects you personally, because you inherited this, you didn't cause it.

**Check the club's actual tax status before you assume you owe a pile of money.** A lot of community sports clubs are not-for-profits, and depending on how yours is set up, some or all of its income may be exempt or treated under mutuality rules (member subscriptions and internal takings often aren't taxable income the way a business's sales are). "Under-reported income" sounds alarming, but the real exposure depends entirely on whether the club was even liable for tax on those amounts. You might be panicking about a number that's a fraction of what you fear. You might also find it's genuinely owed. You won't know until someone who understands NFP tax looks at it.

**Get a tax agent or accountant with not-for-profit experience.** This is the single highest-value move. Not a general bookkeeper, someone who's dealt with clubs and voluntary disclosures. They'll tell you the real dollar figure, whether the club owes anything at all, and they'll handle the disclosure wording, which matters.

**Disclose it yourself, before the tax office finds it.** Voluntary disclosure almost always means dramatically lower penalties, and for genuine mistakes the office is generally reasonable. The advantage of going to them first largely evaporates once they come to you. So the goal is: quantify, then disclose, not sit on it hoping.

**Tell your committee or board in writing.** You're the treasurer of a shared organisation, and this isn't yours to carry alone or to quietly fix. A short factual note to the committee: what was found, what you're doing about it, that you're engaging an accountant. It's a governance duty and it takes the personal weight off you.

Two things to keep steady on. You are not personally liable for the previous treasurer's errors just because you found them, and volunteers acting in good faith are generally protected. And the "two years" isn't as scary as it sounds. Tax offices deal with multi-year amended returns constantly.

If you tell me which country you're in and roughly how the club is structured (incorporated association, registered charity, how it earns money), I can get more specific about the disclosure process and whether the income was even taxable. That's the detail that changes everything here.

23.reply:
First, breathe. You didn't make these errors, you found them. That distinction matters more than it feels like right now.

Here's the straight version.

**You are almost certainly not in personal trouble.** You're a volunteer who inherited someone else's mistakes and is trying to fix them the moment you spotted them. Tax offices treat that situation completely differently from someone hiding income and getting caught. Voluntary disclosure, before they come knocking, is the single biggest thing in your favour, and you're already on that path just by not ignoring it.

**Don't touch the old records.** Don't "correct" the past years' books, don't delete anything, don't rewrite entries to make them look right. Leave the trail exactly as it is. You want to be able to show what was recorded, what actually happened, and the gap between them. Altering records after the fact is the one move that turns an innocent mistake into something that looks deliberate.

**Before you assume the worst, check whether the club even owes income tax.** A lot of community sports clubs are not-for-profits that are exempt from income tax entirely (in Australia, many self-assess as income-tax-exempt sporting organisations). If that's your club, "under-reporting income" may not create an income-tax debt at all. GST is a separate question, and only bites if the club is registered for it. So the actual liability could be far smaller than the raw dollar figure in your head. You don't know yet, and that's fine.

Now the order of operations:

1. **Quantify it, roughly.** Which years, and how far off is each one? You need a ballpark, not a perfect figure, before you can judge how serious this is or talk to anyone about it.

2. **Tell the committee.** This is a club problem, not your private burden. Put it to the president/board in writing, plainly: previous treasurer made errors across two years, income was under-reported, you're getting it assessed. Document that you raised it and when. This protects you and it's the right thing.

3. **Get one hour with an accountant who knows not-for-profits.** Not a mate's opinion, an actual professional. They'll tell you in about twenty minutes whether the club is tax-exempt, whether GST is in play, and what the real exposure is. This is worth the club paying for. If money's tight, many will do a short initial consult cheaply, and some accountants do pro bono work for community clubs.

4. **Then make a voluntary disclosure** if one's actually needed. The accountant handles the mechanics. Penalties get reduced, sometimes to nothing, when you come forward yourself and cooperate. Interest on any genuine shortfall may still apply, but that's a number, not a catastrophe.

On your own liability: a volunteer treasurer acting in good faith, who finds and reports an inherited error, is in a strong position. You're not personally on the hook for the club's tax the way a director might be, and you certainly aren't liable for mistakes made before you took the role. If anyone on the committee tries to make this your fault, the written record from step 2 is your protection.

The thing you're feeling, that this is a disaster, is the panic talking. What you have is a bookkeeping cleanup and probably a disclosure form, handled by a professional, on a timeline of weeks. Not a knock on the door.

Do the rough numbers this week, tell the committee, book the accountant. That's the whole job right now.
