24.reply:
First, breathe. This is fixable, and it's almost certainly not going to fall on you personally. Errors made by someone else, that you found and are moving to correct, are the opposite of the situation the tax office comes down hard on. The people who get hurt are the ones who knew and hid it. You're doing the right thing by moving now.

A few things to hold onto before you act:

**This is the club's issue, not yours.** You inherited a set of books with mistakes in them. Assuming the club is an incorporated association (worth confirming), the liability sits with the entity, not with you as an individual volunteer, as long as you act in good faith. Honest bookkeeping errors are treated completely differently from deliberate concealment. What you have sounds like the former.

**Don't do anything to the records yet.** Don't fix, delete, backdate, or "tidy up" anything in the old files. Preserve them exactly as you found them, including the wrong versions. If you've already started changing things, stop. The trail of what was actually recorded matters, and altering it is the one thing that can turn a mess into something that looks like a cover-up.

Here's the order I'd work in:

1. **Tell your committee first, this week.** Not the tax office yet, your own board or management committee. This is a governance matter and the decision to disclose belongs to the club, not to you alone. Put your discovery in writing to them: what you found, when you found it, roughly how big it looks. That email also protects you, because it dates your discovery and your action.

2. **Get the numbers straight before you talk to anyone official.** You need to know the actual under-reported income for each year before you disclose anything. Don't guess and don't disclose a vague "we think we owe something." That's the accountant's job.

3. **Bring in a registered tax agent or accountant.** This is the part where paying for a couple of hours of professional time is worth it, and the club should cover it, not you. They'll reconstruct the correct figures, tell you exactly which returns need amending, and handle the lodgement. If the club already uses an accountant, start there.

4. **Make a voluntary disclosure, through the agent.** This is the whole reason not to panic. Coming forward before the tax office finds it themselves almost always means sharply reduced penalties, and sometimes none beyond the tax actually owed plus interest. Voluntary correction is a well-worn path; they have a process for exactly this.

On the money: yes, the club will likely owe the under-reported tax plus some interest, possibly a small penalty. That's a real cost, but it's a known, bounded number once the accountant runs it, and it's the club's cost to carry. It is not a catastrophe and it is not coming out of your pocket.

One honest caveat. I'm reading your situation as Australian and as a small incorporated club, which shapes the specifics above (voluntary disclosure, who's liable). If the club is unincorporated, or you're not in Australia, some of the liability detail changes, so tell your accountant the exact structure. But the core moves, preserve records, tell the committee, get the numbers right, disclose voluntarily through a professional, hold in every case.

You found a problem someone else made and you're fixing it properly. That's the job. Write the email to your committee today, and get an accountant on the phone. Everything after that is just process.

25.reply:
First, breathe. You didn't make these errors, you found them. That gap matters more than it feels like right now.

The tax office deals with bookkeeping mistakes all day. Honest error and deliberate fraud sit in completely different worlds, and inherited mistakes that you're voluntarily fixing land firmly on the safe side of that line. The single biggest thing working in your favour is voluntary disclosure: going to them before they come to you. It usually cuts penalties hard, sometimes to nothing on the shortfall itself. The bad outcomes come from knowing and staying quiet, not from the errors.

Here's the order I'd work in.

Don't "clean up" the old records. Leave the historical entries exactly as they are. You want a clear before-and-after that shows what was wrong and what you corrected. Editing past entries, even with good intentions, is the one thing that can make honest errors look deliberate.

Tell your committee now, this week. You're a volunteer, and this is a club problem, not a secret for you to carry alone. Get it in the minutes that you discovered it and raised it. That's both the right governance move and the thing that protects you personally.

Get the numbers straight before you talk to the ATO. Bring in a bookkeeper or accountant who works with not-for-profits and have them quantify the real shortfall across both years. You need to know which income, which periods, and whether this is income tax, GST, or both, before you disclose anything. A guess helps nobody.

Then make the voluntary disclosure through that accountant. They'll know the form and the framing.

Two things worth checking early, because they might shrink this a lot. Many community sporting clubs are income-tax exempt as not-for-profits; if yours is, "under-reported income" may carry little or no income-tax consequence, and the real question shifts to GST or reporting instead. So the scary number in your head may not be the actual number. And on personal exposure: if the club is an incorporated association, the entity is generally liable rather than you as an individual, especially for errors you inherited and reported. Confirm your structure, and check whether the club holds any office-holder or volunteer insurance.

One honest caveat. I'm not your accountant or lawyer, and the specifics turn on your club's structure, your state, and the exact figures. Take this as how to steer, not the final legal read.

Clubs sort out this kind of thing regularly. Get the accountant engaged this week, follow the order above, and you'll be through it.

26.reply:
First, the thing you actually need to hear: this is recoverable, and you're standing on the right side of it. You didn't make these errors. You found them, and you're moving to fix them. Tax offices treat that completely differently from someone who hides it or gets caught in an audit. Voluntary correction before they come looking is the single biggest factor in how this ends, and that lever is still entirely in your hands right now.

So don't panic-act. A few things not to do before you understand the numbers:

- Don't lodge, amend, or pay anything yet.
- Don't "tidy up" or delete the old records. Leave the predecessor's books exactly as they are. Those records are your evidence that this was error, not concealment.

Now, in order:

**Tell the committee this week and get it minuted.** This is a governance matter for the whole board, not something you carry alone or fix quietly. Minuting it does two things: it puts the discovery on record with a date, and it gets the committee to formally authorize spending money on a professional. Do that before anything else.

**Engage a registered tax agent or accountant.** For a small club this is a modest cost and worth every dollar. They quantify the actual shortfall and handle the voluntary disclosure properly, which is where the real risk reduction happens. Ask specifically for someone who works with not-for-profits.

**Pin down the scope.** Which years, roughly how much income, and, critically, what tax actually applies.

That last point is where your panic may be running ahead of the facts. Plenty of community sports clubs are non-profit and self-assess as income-tax exempt, in which case under-reported income may carry little or no income tax at all. The real exposure might instead be something narrower, like GST if the club crossed a registration threshold, or a reporting obligation that was missed. You could be bracing for a bill that is much smaller than it feels tonight, or a different shape entirely.

On personal liability: you're a volunteer who inherited someone else's mistake and acted honestly the moment you found it. That's about the strongest position there is. Keep a short dated note of when you discovered it and every step you took. The exposure sits with the entity, and honest, prompt action is exactly what protects an officeholder.

One question so I can be more precise: what country is the club in, and do you know its tax status (non-profit, income-tax exempt, GST/VAT registered)? Those two facts change the specifics a lot, and I can point you at the right disclosure process from there.

27.reply:
First, breathe. You found this. You didn't cause it, and you're catching it before the tax office did. That distinction matters more than anything else in what follows.

Here's the honest read: honest bookkeeping errors by a previous treasurer are one of the most common things tax authorities deal with from small clubs. It is not fraud, and they treat it very differently from fraud. The thing that turns a manageable correction into a real problem is not the original error. It's what happens after someone discovers it. So the next few steps are about not making it worse.

What to do, roughly in order:

1. **Don't touch the books to "fix" them yourself.** Don't backdate, don't quietly adjust, don't delete anything. Preserve every record exactly as it is, including the errors. If it ever gets looked at, a clean paper trail showing you found a problem and acted properly is your best protection. A tidied-up ledger with no explanation looks like a cover-up even when it isn't.

2. **Write down what you found and when.** A short dated file note: what the errors are, which years, roughly how much, and the date you discovered it. This does two things. It protects you personally, and it becomes the basis for the disclosure later.

3. **Tell the committee, don't carry this alone.** This is the club's liability, not yours personally. You're a volunteer who inherited someone else's mistake. The decision about how to fix it needs to be made by the committee or board on the record, in the minutes. Get it in front of them fast. That both shares the load and shows you acted in good faith the moment you knew.

4. **Get a professional to quantify it.** You need an accountant or registered tax agent to work out the actual shortfall across both years. Do not rely on your own panic-math. The club pays for this, not you. And here's a real possibility worth holding onto: small member-based clubs often have tax concessions, and depending on where you are, income from your own members may not be assessable the same way outside income is. The genuinely taxable shortfall could be a lot smaller than the raw "under-reported" figure looks right now.

5. **Voluntary disclosure is your biggest lever.** Most tax systems have a formal process for coming forward before they catch you, and it usually cuts penalties and interest substantially, sometimes dramatically. Coming to them beats them coming to you by a wide margin. Your accountant handles the mechanics, but the decision to disclose should be made and minuted by the committee, promptly.

On your personal exposure, since I suspect that's the real fear: a volunteer officer acting in good faith, who found an error and reported it up the chain, is in a very different position from someone who hid it. Good faith and prompt action are what keep this off you personally.

One thing I can't do well from here is give you the exact process, because penalties and disclosure rules differ by country. Tell me where the club is based and I'll get specific about who you contact and how the voluntary disclosure actually works there.

You're doing the right thing by not sitting on it. That instinct is correct. Now make it a committee decision, get an accountant on it, and disclose. That path almost always ends somewhere fine.

28.reply:
Breathe. This is recoverable, and you're already doing the thing that matters most: you found it and you're not sitting on it.

First, the distinction that will shape everything. A volunteer predecessor making honest bookkeeping errors is not fraud. You didn't cause this, and you're moving to correct it the moment you found it. Tax offices treat "we made mistakes and came forward" very differently from "we got caught." That good-faith position is your strongest asset, so protect it.

A few things to do, roughly in order.

Don't alter anything. Don't clean up, re-date, or "fix" the old records. Preserve them exactly as they are, including the errors. Write down today's date and what you discovered, in plain terms, and keep that note. If anyone later asks when the club knew, you want a clear answer.

Tell your committee before you tell the tax office. You're a volunteer treasurer, not the sole owner of this problem. The club (its committee or board) needs to know, and the decision to make a disclosure should be minuted as theirs, not carried alone by you. This also covers you personally.

Find out whether the club actually owes what you think it owes. This matters more than the panic is letting you see right now. A lot of small community sports clubs are not-for-profits and are income-tax exempt, either self-assessed or endorsed. If yours is, "under-reported income" may not translate into an income tax bill at all. But there could still be GST, PAYG on any paid staff, or other obligations in play. So the real first question is: what taxes is this club actually registered for, and what's its tax status? You may be scared of a number that doesn't exist.

Get a registered tax agent or accountant, ideally one who works with not-for-profits and community clubs. This is worth paying for even on a small club budget. They'll size the actual exposure, tell you if a voluntary disclosure is needed, and handle the wording. Two years of a volunteer's errors is a completely ordinary thing for them to clean up.

On voluntary disclosure: if money is genuinely owed, coming forward yourself, before any audit, is the single biggest lever on penalties. Most tax offices sharply reduce or waive penalties for voluntary disclosures made in good faith, and often the practical outcome is back-tax plus some interest rather than anything punitive. The accountant will know the current process.

I'm reading your situation as Australian (ATO), given the setup. If the club is somewhere else, say so and the specifics shift, but the shape of the advice holds anywhere.

What would actually help right now: do you know the club's tax registration status, whether it's a registered NFP, and rough figures on how much income went unreported and over which period? If you can pull those together, you'll walk into that accountant's office with the exact things they need, and you'll probably feel a lot less panicked once there's a real number instead of a fear.

You're handling this the right way. Keep going.

29.reply:
First: breathe. You're the person who found the problem, not the person who caused it. That distinction matters, legally and practically.

Here's the straight version.

You almost certainly haven't committed anything. Innocent bookkeeping errors by a predecessor, discovered and voluntarily corrected by the new treasurer, is close to the best-case scenario the ATO sees. The people who get hammered are the ones who hide it or ignore it after finding out. You're doing the opposite. Keep it that way and your downside shrinks a lot.

Before you assume there's even a tax bill, check whether the club owes income tax at all. A lot of small community sporting clubs are non-profits that either self-assess as income-tax-exempt or fall under specific exemptions. If that's your club, "under-reported income" may not translate into unpaid income tax at all. Where it might bite is GST, but only if the club's turnover was over the registration threshold and it was actually registered. So the real number could be much smaller than the panic in your chest right now suggests. Find out what the club's actual tax status is before you catastrophise the size of it.

What to do, in order:

1. **Stop and preserve.** Don't fix, delete, or "clean up" any of the old records yet. Pull together the bank statements, old returns, and whatever the previous treasurer handed over. You want an accurate picture of what was reported versus what actually came in, across both years.

2. **Tell your committee.** You are a volunteer on a committee, not a sole operator. This is the club's problem, not yours to carry alone, and the committee needs it minuted that you raised it. That record also protects you personally. Do this at the next meeting, or call one if it's material.

3. **Get a registered tax agent or accountant.** Ideally one who has worked with not-for-profits or sporting clubs. An hour of their time will tell you three things you can't reliably work out yourself: whether tax was even owed, how much, and whether the club qualifies for the ATO's voluntary disclosure treatment. Many will do a discounted or pro-bono first consult for a small community club. Ask.

4. **Make a voluntary disclosure, through the agent.** Correcting it yourself before the ATO comes knocking usually means reduced or waived penalties and, often, a manageable payment arrangement. The ATO deals with amended community-org returns constantly. This is routine to them even though it feels like the sky is falling to you.

On the personal-liability worry, because it's probably sitting under everything: as a volunteer committee member acting in good faith, you're not on the hook for the club's tax debt out of your own pocket. That protection depends on you acting honestly once you know, which is exactly what these steps are.

One caution. I'm giving you the shape of what to do, not financial or legal advice for your specific club, and I don't know your jurisdiction's fine print or whether the errors were genuinely innocent. If anything you find looks like deliberate concealment rather than sloppiness, that changes the picture and you get the agent involved faster.

Want me to draft the note you'd send your committee, or a plain-English list of exactly which documents to gather first?

30.reply:
First: you're not the one who made the errors, and you found them. That's the difference between a mess and a crime. Tax authorities treat an honest mistake that gets corrected very differently from one that gets hidden. Right now you're in the good category. The main way people fall out of it is by panicking and doing something rushed.

So don't do anything to the records yet. Don't fix, backdate, delete, or "clean up" anything. Preserve every version of the books as they are, including the wrong ones. The paper trail showing this was an inherited error is your protection.

Here's what actually matters, roughly in order.

**Get the real numbers before you tell anyone official anything.** "We think we under-reported for two years" is not something you report. You need the actual figures: what was declared, what should have been, per year. Don't ballpark this to the tax office. Half the time these situations shrink a lot once someone competent goes through them.

**This might not even be taxable income.** A lot of small sports clubs are non-profit or run on the mutuality principle, where money from members (subs, fees, bar takings from members) isn't assessable income at all. If your previous treasurer was counting member contributions as taxable income they should have reported, the "under-reporting" could partly evaporate. An accountant who knows non-profits will spot this fast. Don't assume the worst-case number is the real number.

**Bring in a tax agent or accountant now, ideally one who's handled clubs or charities.** This is the one thing I'd spend money on immediately. A voluntary disclosure done through a professional lands much softer than one you cobble together while stressed, and they'll tell you whether there's even a liability. If the club has almost no cash, some accountants do discounted or pro bono work for community groups. Ask.

**Voluntary disclosure is the lever.** In most jurisdictions (Australia's ATO included), coming forward before they contact you cuts penalties dramatically, sometimes to near zero for a genuine error, and can reduce or remove interest. The value of that lever drops the moment they find you first. That's the real reason not to sit on this for months, not the panic.

**Tell your committee or board, in writing, and minute it.** You have a governance duty to raise it, and doing so on the record protects you personally. You're acting in good faith on something you inherited. Document the date you discovered it and the steps you're taking. Get the committee's agreement to engage an accountant so it's a club decision, not you carrying it alone.

**Check whether the club has insurance or professional indemnity** that covers office-holders. Some do. Worth five minutes.

One more thing, because you said be straight: the emotional weight you're feeling is out of proportion to your actual exposure. You volunteered, you found a predecessor's mistake, and you're moving to fix it correctly. That's the exact behaviour every tax office says it wants. The worst realistic outcome for a small club is back-payment plus modest interest, spread out if needed. The thing that turns this bad is concealment, and you're doing the opposite.

If you tell me the country (and roughly the club's annual turnover and whether it's a registered non-profit), I can point you at the specific disclosure process and whether a threshold likely gets you off the hook entirely.

I can point you at the specific disclosure process"). Two separate violation types, well past the one-violation allowance.
